Question: How will you separate iodine from sodium chloride?
Solution: Either by sublimation or by extraction with CCl4 followed by evaporation.

Question: The X-ray beam coming from an X-ray tube will be
Options: Having all wavelengths smaller than a certain maximum wavelength
Having all wavelengths larger than a certain minimum wavelength
Having all wavelengths lying between a minimum and maximum wavelength.
Solution:
The X-ray beam coming out from an X-ray tube contains more than one wavelength, so it not monochromatic.
The maximum kinetic energy of the X-rays photons is equal to the maximum energy of the electron incident.
Therefore, $\frac{{hc}}{\lambda } = eV$
Therefore, the minimum wavelength of the X-ray beam, $\lambda = \frac{{hc}}{{eV}}$
The minimum kinetic energy of the X-rays photons is zero, the maximum wavelength of the X-ray beam is infinite
Hence, the wavelength for X-ray beam, ${\lambda _{\min }} \leqslant \lambda \leqslant \infty $

Tags.Subject: Physics
Tags.Chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter

Question: Which of the following statement is false for the properties of electromagnetic waves?
Options: both electric and magnetic field vectors are perpendicular to each other and perpendicular to the direction of propagation of wave
The energy in electromagnetic wave is divided equally between electric and magnetic vectors
Both electric and magnetic field vectors are parallel to each other and perpendicular to the direction of propagation of wave
These waves do not require any material medium for propagation
Solution: In an electromagnetic wave, both electric and magnetic field vectors are perpendicular to each other and also perpendicular to the direction of propagation of wave

Question: In the reaction \[_1^2H + \,_1^3H \to \,_2^4He + \,_0^1n,\] if the binding energies of\[_1^2H\],\[\,_1^3H\] and \[_2^4He\] are respectively a, b and c (in MeV), then the energy (in MeV) released in this reaction is
Solution:
The reaction is, ${}_1^2H + {}_1^3H \to {}_2^4He + {}_0^1n$
Now, the mass defect,
$\Delta m = {\text{ mass of product}} - {\text{ mass of reactant}}$
Then, the total energy released, $E = 931\Delta m{\text{ MeV}}$
Now, the binding energy of $\left( {{}_1^2H + {}_1^3H} \right) = a + b$, and ${}_2^4He = c$
As we know that, in a nuclear reaction the resultant nucleus is more stable than the parent nucleus.
Therefore, binding energy E=$c - \left( {a + b} \right)$
$\therefore c - a - b$

Question: A uniform wire of length L, diameter D and density f is stretched under a tension T. The correct relation between its fundamental frequency f, the length L and the diameter D is :
Solution:
Given that,
Frequency = f

Diameter = $$D$$

Length = $$L$$

Tension = $$T$$

Density = $$P$$

Now, we know that

$$f = \frac{c}{\lambda }$$

$$f = \frac{1}{{2L}} \times \sqrt {\frac{T}{\mu }} $$

$$\mu $$is mass per unit length

$$\mu = \rho .\pi .\frac{{{D^2}}}{4}$$

Now, the frequency is

$$f = \frac{1}{{2L}} \times \sqrt {\frac{T}{{\rho .\pi \frac{{{D^2}}}{{4\rho }}}}} $$

$$f \propto \frac{1}{{LD}}$$

Hence, the correct relation is $f\, \propto \,\frac{1}{{LD}}$

Question: What is the numerical value of vector $3\hat i + 4\hat j + 5\hat k?$
Zygotic meiosis occurs in
Solution: Zygotic meiosis occurs in the formation of haploid spores in the haplontic life cycle. It occurs in most algae and the haploid spores undergo mitosis to form haploid gametophytes. The gametophyte is the free-living, dominant phase in organisms with a haplontic life cycle. In Bryophytes, Pteridophytes, and Gymnosperms, the zygote develops through mitosis into the multicellular diploid sporophyte. The meiosis occurs in this diploid sporophyte to produce haploid gametes.

Question: Baculoviruses (Nucleopolyhedrovirus) do not show
Solution: Baculoviruses are pathogens that attack insects and other arthropods. The majority of baculoviruses used as biological control agents are in the genus Nucleopolyhedrovirus. These viruses are excellent candidates for species-specific, narrow spectrum insecticidal applications. They have been shown to have no negative impacts on plants, mammals, birds, fish or even on non-target insects. This is especially desirable when beneficial insects are being conserved to aid in an overall integrated pest management (IPM) programme, or when an ecologically sensitive area is being treated

Question: A particle of mass 1 mg has the same wavelength as an electron moving with a velocity of 3 × 10<sup>6</sup> ms<sup>–1</sup>. The velocity of the particle is (mass of electron = 9.1 × 10<sup>–31</sup> kg)
Solution:
The wavelength of the particle$ = $ the wavelength of an electron Therefore, ${\lambda _{particle}} = {\lambda _{electron}}$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{h}{{{m_p}{v_p}}} = \frac{h}{{{m_e}{v_e}}}$
$ \Rightarrow {m_p}{v_p} = {m_e}{v_e}$
Now, the velocity of the particle, ${v_p} = \frac{{{m_e}{v_e}}}{{{m_p}}}$
$ \Rightarrow {v_p} = \frac{{9.31 \times {{10}^{ - 31}} \times 3 \times {{10}^6}}}{{{{10}^{ - 6}}}}$
$\therefore {v_p} = 2.7 \times {10^{ - 18}}{\text{ m/s}}$

Question: Following forces start acting on a particle at rest at the origin of the co-ordinate system simultaneously \[{\overrightarrow F _1} = 5\hat i - 5\hat j + 5\hat k\], \[{\overrightarrow F _2} = 2\hat i + 8\hat j + 6\hat k\], \[{\overrightarrow F _3} =  - 6\hat i + 4\hat j - 7\hat k\], \[{\overrightarrow F _4} =  - \hat i - 3\hat j - 2\hat k\]. The particle will move
Unit vector parallel to the resultant of vectors $\vec A = 4\hat i - 3\hat j$and $\vec B = 8\hat i + 8\hat j$will be:
Solution:
Resultant of vectors $\overrightarrow A $ and $\overrightarrow B $
$\overrightarrow R = \overrightarrow A + \overrightarrow B = 4\hat i - 3\hat j + 8\hat i + 8\hat j$ $ = 12\hat i + 5\hat j$

$\hat R = \frac{{\overrightarrow R }}{{|R|}} = \frac{{12\hat i + 5\hat j}}{{\sqrt {{{(12)}^2} + {{(5)}^2}} }}$$ = \frac{{12\hat i + 5\hat j}}{{13}}$

Question: The work function of a substance is 4.0 eV. The longest wavelength of light that can cause photoelectron emission from this substance is approximately
Solution:
Work function = 4 eV = 4 × 1.6 × 10–19 J
= 6. 4 × 10–19J

For photoelectron emission photon must have

E ≥ Work function

\[E = \frac{{hc}}{\lambda }\]

\[\frac{{hc}}{\lambda } \geqslant 6.4 \times {10^{ - 19}}\]

\[\frac{{6.626 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}(3 \times {{10}^8})}}{\lambda } \geqslant 6.4 \times {10^{ - 19}}\]

\[\lambda \leqslant \frac{{6.626 \times {{10}^{ - 34}} \times 3 \times {{10}^8}}}{{6.4 \times {{10}^{ - 19}}}}\]

\[\lambda \leqslant 3.10 \times {10^{ - 7}}m = 310\,nm\]
\[\lambda \leqslant 310\,\,nm\]

Question: A vector \[\vec A\] when added to the vector \[\vec B = 3\hat i + 4\hat j\]yields a resultant vector that is in the positive y direction and has a magnitude equal to that of \[\vec B\] . Find the magnitude of $\overrightarrow A $?
Solution:
Let

\[\overrightarrow A = x\widehat i + y\widehat j\]

\[\overrightarrow B = 3\widehat i + 4\widehat j \Rightarrow B = \sqrt {{3^2} + {4^2}} = 5\]

\[\overrightarrow A + \overrightarrow B = \overrightarrow R ;\overrightarrow R \] is in +y direction and has magnitude of $\overrightarrow B .$

\[ \Rightarrow R = 5\widehat j\]

\[\therefore \overrightarrow A = \overrightarrow R - \overrightarrow B = \widehat j - 3\widehat i\]

\[ \Rightarrow A = \sqrt {1 + 9} = \sqrt {10} \]

Question: A torque of 10-5 N-m is required to hold a magnet at 90o with the horizontal component of the earth’s magnetic field. The torque required to hold it at 30o will be
Solution:
The magnet in a magnetic field experiences a torque which rotates the magnet to a position in which the axis of the magnet is parallel to the field.
$$\tau = MB\sin \theta $$
where, M is magnitude of magnetic dipole moment, B is magnitude of magnetic field and $$\theta $$ is the angle between the two.
$$Given,\,{\tau _1} = {10^{ - 5}}N - m,$$
$${\theta _1} = {90^o}\,and\,{\theta _2} = {30^o}.$$
As we know
\[{\tau _1} = MB\sin 90^\circ \,\,\,\,...\left( i \right)\]
\[{\tau _2} = MB\sin {30^o}\,\,\,...\left( {ii} \right)\]
On dividing Eq. (i) by Eq. (ii) , we get
$$\frac{{{\tau _1}}}{{{\tau _2}}} = \frac{{{{10}^{ - 5}}}}{{{\tau _2}}} = \frac{1}{{1/2}}$$
$$ \Rightarrow {\tau _2} = \frac{{{{10}^{ - 5}}}}{2} = \frac{{10}}{2} \times {10^{ - 6}}\, = 5 \times {10^{ - 6}}N - m$$

Question: Which of the following pairs of physical quantities does not have same dimensional formula?
Solution:
(a) Work = Force × displacement =

[MLT−2][L]=[ML2T−2]

Torque = force × distance = [MLT−2][L]=[ML2T−2]

Both have same dimensions.

(b) Angular momentum

L=mvr=[M][LT−1][L]=[ML2T−1]

Planck's constant h = F.s/v (∴ E = hv)

= [MLT−2][L]/[T−1] = [ML2T−1]

Dimensions of h and L are equal.

(c) Tension = force = [MLT−2]

Surface tension = Force/i = [MLT−2]/[L] = [ML0T−2]

Dimensions of both are not same.

(d) Impulse = F × Δt = [MLT−2][T] = [MLT−1]

Momentum = mv = [MLT−1]

Both have same dimensions.

Question: A physical quantity P is described by the relation P = a<sup>1/2</sup> b<sup>2</sup> c<sup>3</sup> d<sup>–4</sup> If the relative errors in the measurement of a,b,c and d respectively, are 2%, 1%, 3% and 5%, then the relative error in P will be-
Solution:
ΔP/P = (1/2)(Δa/a) + 2(Δb/b) + 3(Δc/c) + 4(Δd/d)

= ((1/2) × 2) + (2 × 1) + (3 × 3) + (4 × 5)

= 32%

Question: Two bar magnets having same geometry with magnetic moments M and 2M are placed in such a way that their similar poles are on the same side, then its time period of the oscillation is T<sub>1</sub>. Now, if the polarity of one of the magnets is reversed, then time period of oscillation is T<sub>2</sub>, then
Solution:
Using the formula, for time period,
$$T = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{I}{{MH}}} \Rightarrow T \propto \frac{1}{{\sqrt M }}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...\left( i \right)$$
When similar poles placed at same side, then
$${M_1} = M + 2M = 3M$$
$$So,\,\,from\,\,Eq.\left( i \right)$$
$${T_1} \propto \frac{1}{{\sqrt {3M} }}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,....\left( {ii} \right)$$
When the polarity of a magnet is reversed, then
$${M_2} = 2M - M = M$$
So, from Eq. (i)
$${T_2} \propto \frac{1}{{\sqrt M }}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...\left( {iii} \right)$$
Now, on dividing Eq. (ii) by Eq. (iii), we get
$$\frac{{{T_1}}}{{{T_2}}} = \frac{{\sqrt M }}{{\sqrt {3M} }} = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}$$
$$ \Rightarrow {T_2} = \sqrt 3 \,\,{T_1}$$
Hence, $${T_1} < {T_2}$$

Question: Energy E of a hydrogen atom with principal quantum number n is given by \[E = \frac{{ - 13.6}}{{{n^2}}}\]eV. The energy of a photon ejected when the electron jumps from n = 3 state to n = 2 state of hydrogen, is approximately
Solution:
Energy of a Hydrogen atom, $E = - \frac{{13.6}}{{{n^2}}}{\text{ eV}}$
Now, the energy of photon ejected when the electron jumps from $n = 3$to $n = 2$state of
Hydrogen, $\Delta E = {E_3} - {E_2} = - \frac{{13.6}}{9} - \left( { - \frac{{13.6}}{4}} \right)$
$\therefore \Delta E = - 1.51 + 3.4 = 1.89{\text{ eV}}$
$\therefore \Delta E \approx 1.9{\text{ eV}}$

Question: The type of life-cycle in which there is no free-living sporophyte and the dominant, photosynthetic phase in such plants is the free-living gametophyte. We are talking about
Options: the Haplontic life cycle shown in Volvox and some species of Chlamydomonas
Diplontic life cycle as shown in seed-bearing plants
Haplo-diplontic life cycle as shown in bryophytes and pteridophytes
Haplo-diplontic life cycle as shown in Kelps
Solution:
-The dependent sporophyte on the independent, dominant, photosynthetic gametophyte is represented by the haplontic life cycle in algae such as the Volvox, Spirogyra, and Chlamydomonas.
-The dominant photosynthetic independent phase of the life cycle in seed-bearing plants is the sporophytic generation.
-The sporophytic generation in the haplodiplontic life cycle of both Bryophyta and Pteridophyta are free living.
-Kelps are brown algae and they have a haplontic life cycle where the gametophyte or the sporophyte are almost equally dominant.

Question: A suitable unit for gravitational constant is

\[\therefore G = \frac{{F{r^2}}}{{{m_1}{m_2}}}\]

Substituting the unit of above quantities unit of G = Newton metre2kg−2.

Question: If velocity $v$, acceleration $A$ and force $F$ are chosen as fundamental quantities, then the dimensional formula of angular momentum in terms of $v,\,A$ and $F$ would be
Solution:
Let $L \propto {v^x}{A^y}{F^z}$ $ \Rightarrow $ $L = k{v^x}{A^y}{F^z}$
Putting the dimensions in the above relation
$[M{L^2}{T^{ - 1}}] = k{[L{T^{ - 1}}]^x}{[L{T^{ - 2}}]^y}{[ML{T^{ - 2}}]^z}$
⇒ $[M{L^2}{T^{ - 1}}] = k[{M^z}{L^{x + y + z}}{T^{ - x - 2y - 2z}}]$
Comparing the powers of $M,\,L$ and $T$
$z = 1$ …(i)
$x + y + z = 2$ …(ii)
$ - x - 2y - 2z = - 1$ …(iii)
On solving (i), (ii) and (iii) $x = 3,\,y = - 2,\,z = 1$
So dimension of $L$ in terms of $v,\,A$ and $F$
$[L] = [F{v^3}{A^{ - 2}}]$

Question: The state of the triply ionized beryllium (Be3+) that has the same orbital radius as that of the ground state of hydrogen is
Solution:
Since, radius of nth orbit is given by \[{r_n} \propto \frac{{{n^2}}}{Z}\]
\[{\left( {\frac{{{n^2}}}{Z}} \right)_{{\text{for}}\,{\text{B}}{{\text{e}}^{3 + }}}} = {\left( {\frac{{{n^2}}}{Z}} \right)_{{\text{For}}\,{\text{H}}}}\]
\[\frac{{{n^2}}}{4} = \frac{{{1^2}}}{1} \Rightarrow n = 2\]

Question: In an experiment four quantities a,b,c and d are measured with percentage error 1% , 2 % , 3% and 4% respectively. Quantity P is calculated as follows \[P = \frac{{{a^3}{b^2}}}{{cd}}\]. percentage error in P is
Solution:
Given that
\[P = \frac{{{a^3}{b^2}}}{{cd}}\]
\[\therefore \,\,\frac{{\Delta P}}{P} =  \pm \left[ {3\frac{{\Delta a}}{a} + 2\frac{{\Delta b}}{b} + \frac{{\Delta c}}{c} + \frac{{\Delta d}}{d}} \right]\]
\[ \to \]Percentage error in P,
\[\frac{{\Delta P}}{P} \times 100 = \pm \left[ {3 \times 1\% + 2 \times 2\% + 3\% + 4\% } \right]\]
\[ = \pm 14\% \]
Hence, the % error in P is 14%.

Question: Sodium and copper have work functions 2.3 eV and 4.5 eV respectively. Then the ratio of their threshold wavelengths (for sodium to that for copper) is nearest to
Solution:
Work function $$\left( W \right) = \frac{{hc}}{\lambda }$$

Where, h = Planck's constant,

c = velocity of light 

Therefore, $$\frac{{{W_{Na}}}}{{{W_{Cu}}}} = \frac{{{\lambda _{Cu}}}}{{{\lambda _{Na}}}}$$

$$\frac{{{\lambda _{Na}}}}{{{\lambda _{Cu}}}} = \frac{{{W_{Cu}}}}{{{W_{Na}}}} = \frac{{4.5}}{{2.3}} = 2\left( {{\text{nearly}}} \right)$$

Question:
In the nuclear decay given below
\[_Z^AX\xrightarrow[{}]{}\,_{z + 1}^{\,\,\,\,A}Y\xrightarrow[{}]{}\,_{Z - 1}^{A - 4}{B^*}\xrightarrow[{}]{}\,_{Z - 1}^{A - 4}B,\]
 the particles emitted in the sequence are
Solution:
Nuclear decay reaction,
${}_Z^AX\xrightarrow{\beta }{}_{Z + 1}^AY + {}_{ - 1}^0\beta \xrightarrow{\alpha }{}_{Z - 1}^{A - 4}{B^*} + {}_2^4\alpha \xrightarrow{\gamma }{}_{Z - 1}^{A - 4}B + {}_0^0\gamma $$* \to {\text{excited state}}$
The emission of the beta particle increases the atomic number by one unit and the emission of the alpha particle reduces the mass number by 4 units and atomic number by 2 units, which causes the product element reaches to the excited state to emit gamma particle with no charge and mass number.

Question: Find the value of λ such that the vectors $\overrightarrow a  = 2\widehat i + \lambda \widehat j + \widehat k$ and $\overrightarrow b  = \widehat i + 2\widehat j + 3\widehat k$ are orthogonal
Solution:
Given that $\overrightarrow a = 2\widehat i + \lambda \widehat j + \widehat k$ and $\overrightarrow b = \widehat i + 2\widehat j + 3\widehat k$

Since $\overrightarrow a $ and $\overrightarrow b $ are orthogonal

$\therefore \,\,\,\overrightarrow a \cdot \overrightarrow b = 0$

$ \Rightarrow \,\,\left( {2\widehat i + \lambda \widehat j + \widehat k} \right) \cdot \left( {\widehat i + 2\widehat j + 3\widehat k} \right) = 0$

$ \Rightarrow \,\,2 + 2\lambda + 3 = 0$

$ \Rightarrow \,\,5 + 2\lambda = 0\,\,\, \Rightarrow \,\,\lambda = \frac{{ - 5}}{2}$

Hence, the correct option is (d).

Question: A 200 W sodium street lamp emits yellow light of wavelength 0.6 μm. Assuming it to be 25% efficient in converting electrical energy to light, the number of photons of yellow light it emits per second is:
Solution:
The number of photons of yellow lights emits per second, $n = \frac{P}{E} = \frac{{P\lambda }}{{hc}}$
A $200{\text{ W}}$bulb is $25\% $ efficient, then $P = 200 \times 0.25 = 50{\text{ W}}$
Therefore, $n = \frac{{50 \times 0.6 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}}}{{6.63 \times {{10}^{ - 34}} \times 3 \times {{10}^8}}}$
$\therefore n = 1.5 \times {10^{20}}{\text{ /s}}$

Question: If a magnetic substance is kept in a magnetic field, then which of the following substances is thrown out?
Solution: Diamagnetic substance is repelled by a magnetic field and hence when a diamagnetic substance is placed inside a magnetic field, it is thrown out.

Question: The half-life of ${I^{131}}$ is $8\;days$. Given a sample of ${I^{131}}$ at time $t\; = \;0$, we can assert that
Options: No nucleus will decay before $t\; = \;4$ days
No nucleus will decay before $t\; = \;8$ days
All nuclei will decay before $t\; = \;16$ days
A given nucleus may decay at any time after $t\; = \;0$
Solution: At time $t = 0$, the nuclei start to decay and at any time after that some of the nuclei may have decayed. Up to time $ = 8$ $days$, about half the initial number of the nucleus have decayed and up to 16 days, only one-fourth of the initial number of the nucleus are left undecayed.

Solution: Alkali metals and their salts impart characteristics colour to an oxidizing flame. This is because heat from the flame excites the outermost orbital electron to a higher energy level.

Question: A deuteron is bombarded on $_8O^{16}$ nucleus then $\alpha  - $particle is emitted, then the  product nucleus is
Solution:
The nuclear reaction is
$_8O^{16}{+ _1}{H^2}{ \to _2}H{e^4} + {\,_7}{N^{14}}$

So when a deuteron is bombarded on 8O16 nucleus then an α-particle (2He4) is emitted and the product nucleus is 7N14.

Question: Which type of sexual reproduction is found in Volvox?
Solution: The fusion between one large, non-flagellated female gamete and a smaller flagellated motile male gamete is known as oogamous. Volvox and Fucus are typical examples of oogamy.

Question: A nucleus <sub>n</sub>X<sup>m</sup> emits one α and two β–particles. The resulting nucleus is
Solution:
The emission of $\alpha - $decay decreases the mass number by 4 units and the atomic number by 2 units, and the emission of $\beta - $decay increases the atomic number by one unit but the mass number remains the same.
Hence, the decay will be,
$X_n^m\xrightarrow{\alpha }{}_{n - 2}^{m - 4}Y\xrightarrow{{2\beta }}{}_{n - 2 + 2}^{m - 4}Z$

Question: Hydrogen atom is excited from ground state to another state with principal quantum number equal to 4. Then the number of spectral lines in the emission spectra will be:
Solution:
For ground state, the principal quantum no. $\left( n \right) = 1.$
There is a 3rd excited state for principal quantum number.

The possible number of the spectral lines is given $ = \frac{{n\left( {n - 1} \right)}}{2} = \frac{{4\left( {4 - 1} \right)}}{2} = 6$

Question: If the total magnetic field due to the earth is 28 Am-1., then the total magnetic induction due to the earth is
Solution:
Given, B = 28 Am-1
Magnetic induction B is given by
$$B = {\mu _0}H = 4\pi \times {10^{ - 7}} \times 28$$
$$ = 352 \times {10^{ - 7}}T\,\left( {\because \,\,1\,T = {{10}^4}\,G} \right) = 0.352\,G$$

Question: According to Maxwell’s hypothesis, a changing electric field gives rise to
Zeisel’s method is use for the determination of
Solution:
A method of determining the number of methoxy
(–OCH3) groups in an organic compound. The compound is heated with excess hydroiodic acid, forming an alcohol and iodomethane.
R–O–CH3 + HI \[ \to \] ROH + CH3I
The iodomethane is distilled off and led into an alcoholic solution of silver nitrate, where it precipitates silver iodide. The weight of this AgI precipitate which gives us the number of methoxy groups present. In other words, the number of moles of AgI will be equal to the number of (–OCH3) groups.

Question: The angles which a vector \[\widehat i + \widehat j + \sqrt 2 \widehat k\] makes with X, Y and Z axes respectively are
Solution:
If a vector $\overrightarrow A = a\widehat i + b\widehat j + c\widehat k$ makes angles α, β & γ with x, y and z axes respectively then

$\cos \alpha = \frac{a}{{\sqrt {{a^2} + {b^2} + {c^2}} }},\,\,\cos \beta = \frac{b}{{\sqrt {{a^2} + {b^2} + {c^2}} }}$ and $\cos \gamma = \frac{c}{{\sqrt {{a^2} + {b^2} + {c^2}} }}$

$\therefore \,\,\,\cos \alpha = \frac{1}{{\sqrt {{1^2} + {1^2} + {{\left( {\sqrt 2 } \right)}^2}} }} = \frac{1}{2}\,\,\, \Rightarrow \,\,\alpha = 60^\circ $

$\cos \beta = \frac{1}{{\sqrt {{1^2} + {1^2} + {{\left( {\sqrt 2 } \right)}^2}} }} = \frac{1}{2}\,\,\, \Rightarrow \,\,\beta = 60^\circ $

$\cos \gamma = \frac{{\sqrt 2 }}{{\sqrt {{1^2} + {1^2} + {{\left( {\sqrt 2 } \right)}^2}} }} = \frac{1}{2}\,\,\, \Rightarrow \,\,\gamma = 45^\circ $

Question: Calcium carbonate can be prepared by passing carbon dioxide through…A… or by the addition of sodium carbonate to …B… Here, A and B refer to
Solution:
Calcium carbonate can be prepared by passing CO2 through slaked lime
\[Ca{(OH)_2} + C{O_2}\xrightarrow{{}}CaC{O_3} + {H_2}O\]
Or by addition of sodium carbonate to calcium chloride
\[CaC{l_2} + N{a_2}C{O_3}\xrightarrow{{}}2NaCl + CaC{O_3}\]

Question: Given that P = 12, Q = 5 and R = 13 also \[\vec P + \vec Q = \vec R,\] then the angle between \[\vec P\,\,{\text{and}}\,\,\vec Q\] will be
Solution:
\[\overrightarrow R = \overrightarrow P + \overrightarrow Q ;\,P = 12,Q = 5,R = 13\]

\[{R^2} = {P^2} + {Q^2} + 2PQ\cos \theta \]

\[ \Rightarrow {13^2} = {12^2} + {5^2} + 2\left( {12} \right)\left( 5 \right)\cos \theta \]

\[ \Rightarrow \cos \theta = 0 \Rightarrow \theta = \frac{\pi }{2}\]

Question:
Assertion: The technique of micropropagation has been used to introduce variations in the offspring.
Reason: It is not possible to generate virus-free plants in micropropagation.
Options: If both Assertion and Reason are true, and the Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion
If both Assertion and Reason are true, but the Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion
If assertion is true, but Reason is false
If both Assertion and Reason are false
Solution: Micropropagation is used for rapid vegetative multiplication of plants through plant tissue culture. Each of these plants will be genetically identical to the original plant from which they were grown, i.e. they are somaclones. Hence micropropagation cannot be used to introduce variations in offspring. It is possible to generate virus-free plants through micropropagation by obtaining meristem cells from the plants as meristems are free from viruses.

Question: In an experiment, the following observation’s were recorded: L = 2.820 m, M = 3.00 kg, l = 0.087 cm, Diameter D = 0.041 cm Taking g = 9.81 m/s2 using the formula, \[Y = \frac{{4Mg}}{{\pi {D^2}l}},\] the maximum permissible error in Y is

\[Y = \frac{{4MgL}}{{\pi {D^2}l}}\] so maximum permissible error in

\[Y = \frac{{\Delta Y}}{Y} \times 100 = \left( {\frac{{\Delta M}}{M} + \frac{{\Delta g}}{g} + \frac{{\Delta L}}{L} + \frac{{2\Delta D}}{D} + \frac{{\Delta l}}{l}} \right) \times 100\]

\[ = \left( {\frac{1}{{300}} + \frac{1}{{9.81}} + \frac{1}{{9820}} + 2 \times \frac{1}{{41}} + \frac{1}{{87}}} \right) \times 100\]

\[ = 0.065 \times 100 = 6.5\% \]

Question: Monochromatic light of wavelength 667 nm is produced by a helium neon laser. The power emitted is 9 mW. The number of photons arriving per second on the average at a target irradiated by this beam is
Solution:
The number of photons arriving per second, 

\[n = \frac{P}{E} = \frac{{P \times \lambda }}{{hc}}\]

Where, \[\lambda  = 667nm = 667 \times {10^{ - 9}}m\],

\[P = 9mW = 9 \times {10^{ - 3}}W\]

Therefore, \[n = \frac{{9 \times {{10}^{ - 3}} \times 667 \times {{10}^{ - 9}}}}{{6.6 \times {{10}^{ - 34}} \times 3 \times {{10}^8}}}\]

\[\therefore n = 3 \times {10^{16}}s\]

Product of intercept along Y-axis and mass of the electron
Product of Slope and mass of electron

\[h\nu = {W_o} + c{V_o}\]
\[ \Rightarrow {V_o} = \left( {\frac{h}{e}} \right)\nu + \left( {\frac{{ - {W_o}}}{e}} \right)\]
Comparing with y = mx + c \[ \Rightarrow Slope = \frac{h}{e}\]

Question: A nucleus \[_Z^AX\] has mass represented by m (A, Z). If m<sub>p</sub> and m<sub>n</sub> denotes the mass of proton and neutron respectively and BE the binding energy (in MeV), then
Options: BE = [m (A, Z) – Zm<sub>p</sub> – (A – Z) m<sub>n</sub>] c<sup>2</sup>
BE = [Zm<sub>p</sub> + (A – Z) m<sub>n</sub> – m (A, Z)] c<sup>2</sup>
Be = m (A, Z) – Zm<sub>p</sub> – (A – Z) m<sub>n</sub>
Solution:
${m_p}{\text{ and }}{m_n}$ be the mass of the proton and neutron, respectively. The mass of the nucleus$m\left( {A,Z} \right)$.
The mass defect of the nucleus,
$\Delta m = \left[ {Z{m_p} + \left( {A - Z} \right){m_n} - m\left( {A,Z} \right)} \right]$
Now, the binding energy of a nucleus containing Nneutrons and $Z$ protons, $BE = \Delta m{c^2}$
Therefore,
$BE = \Delta m = \left[ {Z{m_p} + \left( {A - Z} \right){m_n} - m\left( {A,Z} \right)} \right]{c^2}$

Question: The dimensions of universal gravitational constant are (Given, $$F = \frac{{G{m_1}{m_2}}}{{{r^2}}}$$)
If the velocity of light c, universal gravitational constant ${\text{G}}$ and planck's constant ${\text{h}}$ are chosen as fundamental quantities. The dimensions of mass in the new system is:
Solution:
Say dimensional formale of mass is ${{\text{H}}^{\text{x}}}{{\text{C}}^{\text{y}}}{{\text{G}}^{\text{z}}}$
${{\text{M}}^1} = {\left( {{\text{M}}{{\text{L}}^2}{\text{}}{{\text{T}}^{ - 1}}} \right)^{\text{x}}}\left( {{\text{L}}{{\text{T}}^{ - 1}}} \right){\left( {{{\text{M}}^{ - 1}}{\text{}}{{\text{L}}^3}{\text{}}{{\text{T}}^{ - 2}}} \right)^Z}$
${{\text{M}}^1}{\text{}}{{\text{L}}^0}{\text{}}{{\text{T}}^0} = {{\text{M}}^{{\text{x}} - {\text{z}}}}{{\text{L}}^{2{\text{x}} + {\text{y}} + 3z}}{\text{}}{{\text{T}}^{ - {\text{x}} - {\text{y}} - 2{\text{z}}}}$
on comparing both side
${\text{x}} - {\text{z}} = 1$
$2{\text{x}} + {\text{y}} + 3{\text{z}} = 0$
$ - x - y - 2z = 0$
On solving above equations we get
${\text{x}} = \frac{1}{2}{\text{y}} = \frac{1}{2}{\text{z}} = \frac{{ - 1}}{2}$

Question: Order of magnitude of density of uranium nucleus is  \[\left( {{m_p} = 1.67 \times {{10}^{ - 27}}kg} \right)\]
Solution:
Radius of a nucleus is given by
\[R = {R_0}{A^{1/3}}\] (where \[{R_0} = 1.25 \times {10^{ - 15}}m\])
\[ = 1.25{A^{1/3}} \times {10^{ - 15}}m\]
Here A is the mass number and mass of the uranium nucleus will be
\[m = A{m_p}\]where \[{m_p} = \]mass of proton
\[ = A\left( {1.67 \times {{10}^{ - 27}}kg} \right)\]
\[\therefore \,\,\,\]Density \[\rho = \frac{{mass}}{{volume}} = \frac{m}{{\frac{4}{3}\pi {R^3}}}\]
\[ = \frac{{A\left( {1.67 \times {{10}^{ - 27}}kg} \right)}}{{A{{\left( {1.25 \times {{10}^{ - 15}}kg} \right)}^3}}}\,\,\,or\,\,\,\rho = 2.0 \times {10^{17}}\,\,kg/{m^3}\]

Question: Integrated Pest Management (IPM) discourages the excessive use of
Solution: Integrated Pest Management (IPM) discourages the extensive use of chemical pesticides because they cause domestic contamination and death, loss of natural antagonists to pests and pesticide resistance. Biopesticides are the biological agents that are used for the control of weeds, insects, and pathogens. Microorganisms mainly used as biopesticides are viruses, bacteria, protozoa, fungi and mites.

Question: When a gold sheet is bombarded by a beam of α–particles, only a few of them get deflected whereas most go straight, undeflected. This is because
Options: The force of attraction exerted on the α–particles by the oppositely charged electrons is not sufficient.
A nucleus has a much smaller volume than that of an atom.
The force of repulsion acting on the fast-moving α–particles are very small.
The neutrons in the nucleus do not have any effect on the α–particles.
Solution: Only a few get deflected whereas most of them go straight, undeflected because most of the space in the atom is empty and the nucleus has very small volume because of which deflection occurs.

Question: Half-life period of a radioactive substance is 6 h. After 24 h activity is 0.01 μC, what was the initial activity?
Solution:
The half-decay time, $n = \frac{t}{T}$
$ \Rightarrow n = \frac{{24}}{6} = 4$
Now, the half-decay activity of the radioactivity
substance, $\frac{A}{{{A_0}}} = {\left( {\frac{1}{2}} \right)^n}$
Where, final decay activity is, $A = 0.01{\text{ }}\mu {\text{C}}$
Therefore, $0.01 = {A_0}{\left( {\frac{1}{2}} \right)^4}$
$\therefore {A_0} = 0.16{\text{ }}\mu {\text{C}}$

Question: The stable nucleus that has a radius half that of Fe56 is
Solution:
The radius of the nucleus, $R = {r_0}{A^{\frac{1}{3}}}$where, $A$ is the mass number.
Now, $\frac{{{R_1}}}{{{R_2}}} = {\left( {\frac{{{A_1}}}{{{A_2}}}} \right)^{\frac{1}{3}}}$
Therefore, the radius half that of $F{e^{56}}$ is, ${R_1} = R$,
and ${R_2} = \frac{R}{2}$, and ${A_1} = 56$
$ \Rightarrow 2 = {\left( {\frac{{56}}{{{A_2}}}} \right)^{\frac{1}{3}}}$
$\therefore {A_2} = \frac{{56}}{8} = 7$
Hence, the stable nucleus that has a radius half that of $F{e^{56}}$ is $L{i^7}$.

Question: The magnetic susceptibility of a paramagnetic substance at –73°C is 0.0060, then its value at –173°C will be

According to Bohr’s theory of hydrogen atom, for the electron in the nth allowed orbit the
I. linear momentum is proportional to 1/n.
II. radius is proportional to n.
III. kinetic energy is proportional to 1/n2 .
IV. angular momentum is proportional to n.
Choose the correct option from the codes given below
Solution:
According to Bohr’s theory of hydrogen atom, we know that,
Angular momentum, \[mvr = \frac{{nh}}{{2\pi }}\]
\[ \Rightarrow mv = \frac{{nh}}{{2\pi r}}\]
Linear momentum, \[P = mv = \frac{{nh}}{{2\pi r}}\]
\[ \Rightarrow p \propto \frac{n}{r}\]
\[As,r \propto {n^2}\]
\[Hence,{\mkern 1mu} P \propto \frac{1}{n}\]
We know that, kinetic energy
\[KE \propto \frac{1}{r}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} and{\mkern 1mu} r \propto {n^2}\]
\[Hence,\,KE \propto \frac{1}{{{n^2}}}\]

Question: The angle between two vectors $\overrightarrow a $ and $\overrightarrow b $ with magnitudes $\sqrt 3 $ and 4, respectively, and $\overrightarrow a .\overrightarrow b  = 2\sqrt 3 $ is
Solution:
Here, given that \[\left| {\overrightarrow a } \right| = \sqrt 3 ,\,\,\left| {\overrightarrow b } \right| = 4\] and \[\overrightarrow a \cdot \overrightarrow b = 2\sqrt 3 \]

∴ From scalar product, we know that

\[\overrightarrow a \cdot \overrightarrow b = \left| {\overrightarrow a } \right|\left| {\overrightarrow b } \right|\cos \theta \]

$ \Rightarrow \,\,2\sqrt 3 = \sqrt 3 \cdot 4 \cdot \cos \theta $

$ \Rightarrow \,\,\cos \theta = \frac{{2\sqrt 3 }}{{\sqrt 3 \cdot 4}} = \frac{1}{2}$

$\therefore \,\,\,\theta = \frac{\pi }{3}$

Hence, the correct option is (b).

Question: The shortest wavelength of X- rays emitted from an X-ray tube depends upon
Options: nature of the gas in the tube
the voltage applied to the tube
nature of the target of the tube
Solution:
The shortest wavelength of X-rays emitted from an X-ray tube depends primarily on the voltage applied to the tube and the nature of the target material within the tube.
1. Voltage applied to the tube: The wavelength of X-rays produced in an X-ray tube is inversely proportional to the voltage applied across the tube. As the voltage increases, the wavelength of the X-rays decreases, resulting in shorter wavelengths (higher energy X-rays).
2. Nature of the target material: When high-energy electrons produced by the acceleration of electrons in the X-ray tube strike the target material, they undergo rapid deceleration, producing X-rays through bremsstrahlung radiation. A higher atomic number target material results in more efficient production of higher energy X-rays, which have shorter wavelengths.
3. The nature of the gas in the tube and the current in the tube do not directly affect the shortest wavelength of X-rays emitted from an X-ray tube. The gas in the tube is typically used to create a vacuum environment for efficient electron acceleration, and the current in the tube controls the number of electrons flowing through it, affecting the intensity of X-ray emission but not the wavelength.

Question: During a nuclear fusion reaction,
Options: a heavy nucleus breaks into two fragments by itself
a light nucleus bombarded by thermal neutrons breaks up
a heavy nucleus bombarded by thermal neutrons breaks up

Solution: During a nuclear fusion reaction, two or more lighter nuclei fuse together to form one or more different heavier nuclei and release an enormous amount of energy and subatomic particle also. The difference in product and reactant mass is manifested as the release or absorption of energy.

Question: The molecular mass of a organic compound which contains only one nitrogen atom can be

Question: Following figure shows two processes A and B for a gas. If ∆QA and ∆QB are the amount of heat absorbed by the system in two cases, and ∆UA and ∆UB are changes in internal energies respectively, then
Solution:
According to the first law of thermodynamics,
Heat supplied (∆Q) = Work done (W) + Change in internal energy of the system (∆U)
\[\Delta {Q_A} = \Delta {U_A} + {W_A}\]
Similarly, for process B,
\[\Delta {Q_B} = \Delta {U_B} + {W_B}\]
Now, we know that, work done for a process = area under it’s p-V curve
Here,

Thus, it is clear from the above graphs,
\[{W_A} > {W_B}\,\,\,...\left( i \right)\]
Also, since the initial and final state are same in both process, so
\[\Delta {U_A} = \Delta {U_B}\,\,\,...\left( {ii} \right)\]
So, from Eqs. (i) and (ii), we can conclude that
\[\Delta {Q_A} > \Delta {Q_B}\]

Question: When an ideal gas $\left( {\gamma  = 5/3} \right)$is heated under constant pressure, when what percentage of given heat energy will be utilised in doing external work
Solution:
$\Delta Q = \Delta U + \Delta W$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{\Delta W}}{{\Delta Q}} = 1 - \frac{{\Delta U}}{{\Delta Q}} = 1 - \frac{{n{C_V}dT}}{{n{C_P}dT}}$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{\Delta W}}{{\Delta Q}} = 1 - \frac{{{C_V}}}{{{C_P}}} = 1 - \frac{3}{5} = \frac{2}{5} = 0.4 = 40\% $

Question: If $3x - 5y = 2x + y,$ find the value of $\frac{{\left( {3x + 5y} \right)}}{{\left( {3x - 5y} \right)}}$?
Solution:
$ \Rightarrow \,\,3x - 5y = 2x + y$

$ \Rightarrow \,\,3x - 2x = 5y + y$

$ \Rightarrow \,\,x = 6y$

$ \Rightarrow \,\,\frac{x}{y} = \frac{6}{1}$

Multiplying both side by 3 and dividing by 5 we get,

$ \Rightarrow \,\,\frac{{3x}}{{5y}} = \frac{{18}}{5}$

Using componendo and dividendo we get

$ \Rightarrow \,\,\frac{{\left( {3x + 5y} \right)}}{{\left( {3x - 5y} \right)}} = \frac{{\left( {18 + 5} \right)}}{{\left( {18 - 5} \right)}}$

$\therefore \,\,\,\frac{{\left( {3x + 5y} \right)}}{{\left( {3x - 5y} \right)}} = \frac{{23}}{{13}}$

Question: If the present units of length, time and mass (m, s, kg) are changed to 100m, 100s and \[\frac{1}{{10}}kg\] then
Options: The new unit of velocity is increased 10 times
The new unit of force is decreased \[\frac{1}{{1000}}\] times
The new unit of energy is increased 10 times
The new unit of pressure is increased 1000 times
Solution:
Unit of velocity = m/sec; in new system

\[ = \frac{{100\;m}}{{100\;\sec }} = \frac{m}{{\sec }}\left( {{\text{same}}} \right)\]

Unit of force \[ = \frac{{kg \times m}}{{{{\sec }^2}}};\] in new system

\[ = \frac{1}{{10}}kg \times \frac{{100\;m}}{{100\;\sec \times 100\sec }} = \frac{1}{{1000}}\frac{{kg \times m}}{{{{\sec }^2}}}\]

Unit of energy \[ = \frac{{kg \times {m^2}}}{{{{\sec }^2}}};\] in new system

\[ = \frac{1}{{10}}kg \times \frac{{100\;m \times 100\;m}}{{100\;\sec \times 100\;\sec }} = \frac{1}{{10}}\frac{{kg \times {m^2}}}{{{{\sec }^2}}}\]

Unit of pressure \[ = \frac{{kg}}{{m \times {{\sec }^2}}};\] in new system

\[ = \frac{1}{{10}}kg \times \frac{1}{{100\;m}} \times \frac{1}{{100\;\sec \times 100\;\sec }} = {10^{ - 7}}\frac{{kg}}{{m \times {{\sec }^2}}}\]

\[4\left( {_2H{e^4}} \right) = {}_8{O^{16}}\]
Mass defect, \[\Delta m = \left\{ {4\left( {4.0026} \right) - 15.9994} \right\} = 0.011\]amu
\[\therefore \,\,\,\]Energy released per oxygen nuclei,
\[ = \left( {0.011} \right)\left( {931.48} \right)\,\,MeV = 10.24\,\,MeV\]

Question: Full form of BOD is
Solution: Biochemical oxygen demand (BOD) is the amount of dissolved oxygen that would be consumed if all the organic matter in one liter of water is oxidized by microorganisms at a temperature of 20-degree Celsius. BOD is an indicator of water quality. The high value of BOD indicates that the water is highly polluted by organic matter whereas the low value of BOD indicates that the water is either normal or less polluted by organic matter.

Question: A process in which nutrient enriched water bodies support a dense plant pollutation, which kills animal life by depriving it of oxygen is known as …A…Here, A refers to :
Solution: Eutrophication A process in which nutrient enriched water bodies support a dense plant population, which kills animal life by depriving it of oxygen and results in subsequent loss of biodiversity is known as Eutrophication.

Question:
Ionization potential of hydrogen atom is 13.6 V.
Hydrogen atoms in the ground state are excited by monochromatic radiation of photon energy 12.1 eV. The spectral lines emitted by hydrogen atoms according to Bohr's theory will be
Solution:
$IE = 13.6eV$

Excited photon energy 12.1 eV

The energy of the atom at excited state

$13.6 - 12.1 = 1.5eV$

$\therefore {E_n} = 1.5eV$

$E \propto \frac{1}{{{n^2}}} \Rightarrow {E_n} = \frac{{JE}}{{{n^2}}}$

$1.5 = \frac{{13.6}}{{{n^2}}}$

${n^2} = \frac{{13.6}}{{1.5}} = 9$

$n = 3$

Question: A magnet 10 cm long and having a pole strength 2 A-m is deflected through 30o from the magnetic meridian. Here the axis of rotation is vertical, the horizontal component of earth’s induction is 0.32 × 10-4 T, then the value of deflecting couple is
Solution:
As, couple acting on the bar magnet is given as
$$\tau = M{B_H}\sin \theta $$
$$\tau = \left( {2l} \right)m\,\,{B_H}\,\sin \theta $$
$$Given,\,2l = 10\,cm = 10 \times {10^{ - 2}}m,$$
$$m = 2\,A - m,\,\theta = {30^o}\,and\,{B_H} = 0.32 \times {10^{ - 4}}T$$
Putting these values, we get
$$\tau = 10 \times {10^{ - 2}}\left( 2 \right)\,\left( {0.32 \times {{10}^{ - 4}}} \right) \times \frac{1}{2}$$
$$\tau = 32 \times {10^{ - 7}}\,N - m$$

Question: An electron jumps from the 4<sup>th</sup> orbit to the 2<sup>nd</sup> orbit of hydrogen atom. Given the Rydberg's constant $R = {10^5}c{m^{ - 1}}$. The frequency (in Hz) of the emitted radiation will be

Question: According to Joule's law of heating, heat produced $H = {I^2}$Rt, where I is current, R is resistance and t is time. If the errors in the measurement of I, R and t are 3%, 4% and 6% respectively then error in the measurement of H is
Solution:
$H = {I^2}R\,t$
$\therefore \,\,\,\frac{{\Delta H}}{H} \times 100 = \left( {\frac{{2\Delta I}}{I} + \frac{{\Delta R}}{R} + \frac{{\Delta t}}{t}} \right) \times 100$ $ = (2 \times 3 + 4 + 6)\% $ $ = 16\% $

   = \frac{{{N_0}}}{4}\xrightarrow{{{\text{5}}\,{\text{years}}}}\frac{{{N_0}/4}}{2} = \frac{{{N_0}}}{8} \\
\end{gathered} \]

Question: The energy of a photon of light is 3 eV. Then the wavelength of photon is nearly
Solution:
The energy of a photon of light,
$E = \frac{{hc}}{\lambda } = 3{\text{ eV}} = 3 \times 1.602 \times {10^{ - 19}}{\text{ V}}$
Now, the wavelength of the photon, $\lambda = \frac{{hc}}{E}$
$ \Rightarrow \lambda = \frac{{6.63 \times {{10}^{ - 34}} \times 3 \times {{10}^8}}}{{3 \times 1.602 \times {{10}^{ - 19}}}}$
$\therefore \lambda = 4.138 \times {10^{ - 7}}{\text{ m}} = 413.8{\text{ nm}}$

Question: If p represents radiation pressure, C represents speed of light and q represents radiation energy striking a unit area per second, then non–zero integers a, b and c are such that p<sup>a</sup> q<sup>b</sup> C<sup>c</sup> is dimensionless, then
Solution:
\[\begin{gathered}
  {\text{Radiation}}\,{\text{pressure}}\,\,[p] = \left[ {\frac{{\text{F}}}{{\text{A}}}} \right] = \left[ {\frac{{{{\text{M}}^{\text{I}}}{{\text{L}}^1}\;{{\text{T}}^{ - 2}}}}{{\;{{\text{L}}^2}}}} \right] \\
  \therefore [{\text{p}}] = \left[ {{{\text{M}}^1}\;{{\text{L}}^{ - 1}}\;{{\text{T}}^{ - 2}}} \right] \\
  {\text{Radiation}}\,{\text{energy}}\,{\text{per}}\,{\text{unit}}\,{\text{area}}\,{\text{per}}\,{\text{sec}}\,\,[{\text{q}}] = \left[ {\frac{{{{\text{M}}^1}\;{{\text{L}}^2}\;{{\text{T}}^{ - 2}}}}{{\left[ {\;{{\text{L}}^2}} \right][{\text{T}}]}}} \right] \\
  \therefore [{\text{q}}] = \left[ {{{\text{M}}^1}\;{{\text{L}}^0}\;{{\text{T}}^{ - 3}}} \right] \\
  Speed\,of\,light\,[C] = \left[ {{{\text{M}}^0}\;{{\text{L}}^1}\;{{\text{T}}^{ - 1}}} \right] \\
  {\text{A}}{\text{.T}}{\text{.Q}}{\text{.}} \\
  {[{\text{p}}]^{\text{a}}}{[{\text{q}}]^{\text{b}}}{[{\text{C}}]^{\text{c}}} = \left[ {{{\text{M}}^0}\;{{\text{L}}^0}\;{{\text{T}}^0}} \right] \\
  {\left[ {{{\text{M}}^1}\;{{\text{L}}^{ - 1}}\;{{\text{T}}^{ - 2}}} \right]^{\text{a}}}{\left[ {{{\text{M}}^{\text{I}}}{{\text{L}}^0}\;{{\text{T}}^{ - 3}}} \right]^{\text{b}}}{\left[ {{{\text{M}}^0}\;{{\text{L}}^1}\;{{\text{T}}^{ - 1}}} \right]^{\text{c}}} = \left[ {{{\text{M}}^0}\;{{\text{L}}^0}\;{{\text{T}}^0}} \right] \\
  \left[ {{{\text{M}}^{{\text{a}} + {\text{b}}}}{{\text{L}}^{ - a + c}}\;{{\text{T}}^{ - 2{\text{a}} - 3\;{\text{b}} - {\text{c}}}}} \right] = \left[ {{{\text{M}}^0}\;{{\text{L}}^0}\;{{\text{T}}^0}} \right] \\
  \therefore {\text{a}} + {\text{b}} = 0 \\
   \Rightarrow {\text{b}} = - {\text{a}} \\
   - 2{\text{a}} - 3\;{\text{b}} - {\text{c}} = 0 \\
  {\text{Put value - 2 a}}\,{\text{ + }}\,{\text{3a}}\,{\text{ - }}\,{\text{a}}\,{\text{ = }}\,{\text{0}} \\

Question: Two radioactive materials X<sub>1</sub> and X<sub>2</sub> have decay constants 5λ and λ respectively. If initially they have the same number of nuclei, then the ratio of the number of nuclei of X<sub>1</sub> to that of X<sub>2</sub> will be \[\frac{1}{e}\] after a time
Solution:
The number of radioactive nuclei, $N = {N_0}{e^{ - \lambda t}}$
Now, ${\lambda _1} = 5\lambda {\text{, and }}{\lambda _2} = \lambda $
Therefore, $\frac{{{N_1}}}{{{N_2}}} = {e^{ - \left( {5\lambda - \lambda } \right)t}} = {e^{ - 4\lambda t}}$
Where, $\frac{{{N_1}}}{{{N_2}}} = \frac{1}{e}$(given)
Therefore, $\frac{1}{e} = {\left( {\frac{1}{e}} \right)^{4\lambda t}}$
$\therefore 4\lambda t = 1 \Rightarrow t = \frac{1}{{4\lambda }}$

Question: During the propagation of electromagnetic waves in a medium: [2014]

Hysteresis loops for two magnetic materials A and B are given below :

These materials are used to make magnets for electric generators, transformer core and electromagnet core. Then it is proper to use:
Options: A for electromagnets and B for electric generators.
A for transformers and B for electric generators.
A for electric generators and transformers.

In loop A, the area of hysteresis loop is more, this means a 'hard' ferromagnetic material.
In loop B, the area of hysteresis loop is less, this means a 'soft' ferromagnetic material.

Question: If the kinetic energy of the particle is increased to 16 times to its previous value, the percentage change in the de-Broglie wavelength of the particle is
Solution:
De - Brogile wavelength -

\[\lambda  = \frac{h}{p} = \frac{h}{{mv}} = \frac{h}{{\sqrt {2mE} }}\]

-wherein

h =  plank's constant

m = mass of particle

v = speed of the particle

E = Kinetic energy of particle

Kinetic energy \[ = \frac{{{p^2}}}{{2m}} = \frac{{{h^2}}}{{2m{\lambda ^2}}}\]

or  \[{\lambda ^2} = \frac{{{h^2}}}{{2mE}}\] (E = kinetic energy)

when E becomes 16 E. \[\lambda \]  becomes  \[{\lambda '}\]

\[{{\lambda '}^2} = \frac{{{h^2}}}{{2m\left( {16E} \right)}} = \frac{1}{{16}}{\lambda ^2}\]

\[\lambda ' = \frac{\lambda }{4}\]

Change in wavelength \[ = \lambda  - \lambda ' = \frac{{3\lambda }}{4}\]

\[\therefore \,\] % change \[ = \frac{{{\text{change}}\,\,{\text{in}}\,\,\lambda }}{\lambda } \times 100\%  = 75\% \]

Question: The heat generated in a circuit is dependent upon the resistance, current and time for which the current is flown. If the errors in measuring the above are as 1 %, 2 % and 1 % the maximum error in measuring heat will be
Solution:
\[{\text{Heat}}\,\left( H \right) = {I^2}Rt\]

\[\left( {\frac{{\Delta H}}{H} \times 100} \right)\]

\[ = \pm \left( {\frac{{2\Delta I}}{I} \times 100 + \frac{{\Delta R}}{R} \times 100 + \frac{{\Delta t}}{t} \times 100} \right)\]

\[ = \pm \left( {2\left( {2\% } \right) + 1\% + 1\% } \right)\]

\[ = \pm \left( {4 + 1 + 1} \right)\% \]

\[ = \pm 6\% \]

Question: A simple pendulum is being used to determine the value of gravitational acceleration g at a certain place. The length of the pendulum is 25.0 cm and a stopwatch with 1s resolution measures the time taken for 40 oscillations to be 50 s. The accuracy in measurement of g is
Solution:
Given that

L = 25cm, Δt = 1s, n = 40, t = 50s

Time period of simple pendulum is given by

\[T = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{L}{g}} \]

$ \Rightarrow g = 4{\pi ^2}L/{t^2}$

⇒ Δg/g = (ΔL/L + 2Δt/t) = (0.1/25.0) + (2 x 1/50) = (1.1/25)

⇒ (Δg/g) × 100% = 4.4%

Question: Which of the following transitions in hydrogen atoms emit photons of highest frequency ?
Solution:
We have no find the frequency of emitted photons. For emission of photons the transition must take place from a higher energy level to a lower energy level which are given only in options (c) and (d).
Frequency is given by
$hv = - 13.6\left( {\frac{1}{{n_2^2}} - \frac{1}{{n_1^2}}} \right)$

For transition from n = 6 to n = 2
${v_1} = \frac{{ - 13.6}}{h}\left( {\frac{1}{{{6^2}}} - \frac{1}{{{2^2}}}} \right)$
$ = \frac{2}{9} \times \left( {\frac{{13.6}}{h}} \right)$

For transition from n = 2 to n = 1,
${v_2} = \frac{{ - 13.6}}{h}\left( {\frac{1}{{{2^2}}} - \frac{1}{{{1^2}}}} \right)$
$ = \frac{3}{4} \times \left( {\frac{{13.6}}{h}} \right)$
$\therefore {v_1} > {v_2}$

Question: The area of the acceleration-displacement curve of a body gives:

We know Newton’s 3rd equation of motion.
\[ \Rightarrow {v^2} = {u^2} + 2as\]

We can further write it as:

\[ \Rightarrow as = \frac{1}{2}\left( {{v^2} - {u^2}} \right)\]

Thus, area under the acceleration-displacement curve gives the change in kinetic energy per unit mass.

The following observations were taken for determining surface tension T of water by capillary method. Diameter of capillary,$d = 1.25 \times {10^{ - 2}}m$ rise of water, \[h = 1.45 \times {10^{ - 2}}m.\]Using g = 9.80 m/s2 and the simplified relation $T = \frac{{rhg}}{2} \times {10^3}\,N/m,$the possible error in surface tension is closest to (2017 Main)

By ascent formula, we have surface tension,

\[\begin{gathered}
  T = \frac{{rhg}}{2} \times {10^3}\frac{N}{m} \\

\[ \Rightarrow \,\,\frac{{\Delta T}}{T} = \frac{{\Delta d}}{d} + \frac{{\Delta h}}{h}\][given, g is constant]
So, percentage \[ = \frac{{\Delta T}}{T} \times 100 = \left( {\frac{{\Delta d}}{d} + \frac{{\Delta h}}{h}} \right) \times 100\]
\[\begin{gathered}
   = \left( {\frac{{0.01 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}}}{{1.25 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}}} + \frac{{0.01 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}}}{{1.45 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}}}} \right) \times 100 = 1.5\% \\
  \therefore \,\,\,\frac{{\Delta T}}{T} \times 100 = 1.5\% \\
\end{gathered} \]

Question: In photoelectric effect, the work function of a metal is 3.5 eV. The emitted electrons can be stopped by applying a potential of –1.2 V. Then
Options: The energy of the incident photons in 4.7 eV
the energy of the incident photons is 2.3 eV
if higher frequency photons be used, the photoelectric current will rise
when the energy of photons is 3.5 eV, the photoelectric current will be maximum
Solution:
The work function of the metal, $\phi = 3.5{\text{ eV}}$,
Stopping potential, $P = - 1.2{\text{ V}}$
Where, $K.E = \left| {P.d.(e)} \right| = 1.2{\text{ eV}}$
Now, according to Einstein’s photoelectric equation,
$K.E = E - \phi $
$ \Rightarrow K.E = \phi + hf$
$ \Rightarrow 1.2 = 3.5 + hf$
$\therefore hf = 4.7{\text{ eV}}$

Question: The resultant of two vectors \[\vec A\,\,and\,\,\vec B\] is perpendicular   to the vector\[\vec A\]  and its magnitude is equal to half of the magnitude of vector \[\vec B.\] Then the angle between \[\vec A\] and \[\vec B\] is
Solution:
\[\overrightarrow R = \overrightarrow A + \overrightarrow B \,;\,\,\overrightarrow R \,\,{\text{is}}\,\,{\text{perpendicular}}\,\,{\text{to}}\,\,\overrightarrow A \]

\[ \Rightarrow \tan \alpha = \frac{{B\sin \theta }}{{A + B\cos \theta }} \Rightarrow A + B\cos \theta = 0\]

\[ \Rightarrow \cos \theta = \frac{{ - A}}{B}\]

\[R = \frac{B}{2} \Rightarrow {R^2} = {A^2} + {B^2} + 2AB\cos \theta \]

\[ \Rightarrow \frac{{{B^2}}}{4} = {A^2} + {B^2} - 2{A^2}\]

\[ \Rightarrow {A^2} = \frac{{3{B^2}}}{4} \Rightarrow \frac{A}{B} = \frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{2}\]

\[ \Rightarrow \cos \theta = \frac{{ - \sqrt 3 }}{2} \Rightarrow \theta = {150^0}\]

Question: The angle between \[\vec P\, + \,\,\vec Q\,\,{\text{and}}\,\,\vec P - \vec Q\] will be
Solution:
In limiting case,

If $\overrightarrow P $ is null vector then

$\overrightarrow P + \overrightarrow Q = \overrightarrow Q $

$\overrightarrow P - \overrightarrow Q = - \overrightarrow Q $

Angle between them is 180°

if $\overrightarrow Q $ is null vector,

$\overrightarrow P + \overrightarrow Q = \overrightarrow P $

$\overrightarrow P - \overrightarrow Q = \overrightarrow P $

Angle between them is 0°

Hence angle between $\overrightarrow P + \overrightarrow Q $ and $\overrightarrow P - \overrightarrow Q $ can range between 0° and 180°

Question: Which of the following transitions gives photon of maximum energy?
Solution:
The energy of electrons, at $n = 1$; $E = - \frac{{13.6}}{{{n^2}}}$
$ \Rightarrow E = - 13.6{\text{ eV}}$
At n = 2, then $E = - \frac{{13.6}}{4} = - 3.4{\text{ eV}}$
At $n = 6$, then $E = - \frac{{13.6}}{{36}} = - 0.37{\text{ eV}}$
Energy is absorbed for the case$n = 1{\text{ to }}n = 2$ and for the case$n = 2{\text{ to }}n = 6$
For the case $n = 2{\text{ to }}n = 1$, the energy released
$E = - 3.4 - \left( { - 13.6} \right) = 10.2{\text{ eV}}$
For the case$n = 6{\text{ to }}n = 2$, the energy released is
$E = - 0.37 - \left( { - 3.4} \right) = 3.03{\text{ eV}}$
Therefore, the photon of maximum energy is emitted when transition is from $n = 2{\text{ to }}n = 1$.

Question: A student measures the time period of 100 oscillations of a simple pendulum four times. The data set is 90 s, 91s, 92 s and 95 s. If the minimum division in the measuring clock is 1s, then the reported mean time should be
Solution:
True value $$ = \frac{{90 + 91 + 95 + 92}}{4} = 92$$

Mean absolute error

$$ = \frac{{\left| {92 - 90} \right| + \left| {92 - 91} \right| + \left| {92 - 95} \right| + \left| {92 - 92} \right|}}{4}$$

$$ = \frac{{2 + 1 + 3 + 0}}{4} = 1.5$$

Value $$ = \left( {92 \pm 1.5} \right)$$

Since, least count is 1 sec

$$\therefore $$ Value $$ = \left( {92 \pm 2s} \right)$$

The pressure on a square plate is measured by measuring the force on the plate and the length of the sides of the plate. If the maximum error in the measurement of force and length are respectively 4% and 2%, The maximum error in the measurement of pressure is

so maximum percentage error in pressure (P)

Question: A sample of radioactive elements contains 4 × 10<sup>10</sup> active nuclei. If half-life of element is 10 days, then the number of decayed nuclei after 30 days is
Solution:
The half-decay time, $n = \frac{t}{T}$$ \Rightarrow n = \frac{{30}}{{10}} = 3$
Now, the number of nuclei decayed, $\frac{N}{{{N_0}}} = {\left( {\frac{1}{2}} \right)^n}$
Where, the initial number of radioactive nuclei,
${N_0} = 4 \times {10^{10}}$
Therefore, $N = 4 \times {10^{10}}{\left( {\frac{1}{2}} \right)^3}$
$ \Rightarrow N = 5 \times {10^9}$
Now, the number of decayed nuclei after $30$ days
is, $ = {N_0} - N = 4 \times {10^{10}} - 5 \times {10^9}$
$\therefore = 3.5 \times {10^{10}}$ decayed nuclei.

Question: If force [F], acceleration [A] and time [T] are chosen as the fundamental physical quantities. Find the dimensions of energy.

\[\left[ {{M^1}{L^2}{T^{ - 2}}} \right] \propto {\left[ {{M^1}{L^1}{T^{ - 2}}} \right]^a}{\left[ {L{T^{ - 2}}} \right]^b}{\left[ T \right]^c}\]

\[a = 1\]

\[a + b = 2\]

\[ \Rightarrow \,\,b = 1\]

\[ - 2a - 2b + c = - 2\]

\[ \Rightarrow \,\,c = 2\]

\[a = 1\]

\[b = 1\]

\[c = 2\]

Question: If $\left( {4a + 9b} \right) \times \left( {4c - 9d} \right) = \left( {4a - 9b} \right)\left( {4c + 9d} \right),$ then the value of a : b can be equal to:
Solution:
Here we have:

$\left( {4a + 9b} \right) \times \left( {4c - 9d} \right) = \left( {4a - 9b} \right)\left( {4c + 9d} \right).$ In other words we can say that: $\frac{{\left( {4a + 9b} \right)}}{{\left( {4a - 9b} \right)}} = \frac{{\left( {4c + 9d} \right)}}{{\left( {4c - 9d} \right)}}$

Hence we have:

$\frac{{\left\{ {\left( {4a + 9b} \right) + \left( {4a - 9b} \right)} \right\}}}{{\left\{ {\left( {4a + 9b} \right) - \left( {4a - 9b} \right)} \right\}}} = \frac{{\left\{ {\left( {4c + 9d} \right) + \left( {4c - 9d} \right)} \right\}}}{{\left\{ {\left( {4c + 9d} \right) - \left( {4c - 9d} \right)} \right\}}}$

[By componendo and dividendo]

In other words we have: $\frac{{8a}}{{18b}} = \frac{{8c}}{{18d}}.$

or we may writer: $\frac{a}{b} = \frac{c}{d}$ i.e. $a:b::c:d.$

Hence $a:b = c:d$ and the correct option here is (A) $c:d.$ Let us see another example below.

Question: The vector sum of two forces is perpendicular to their vector differences. In that case, the force
Options: Are equal to the each other
Are equal to each other in magnitude
Are not equal to each other in magnitude
Solution:
\[\overrightarrow P = \,{\text{vector}}\,{\text{sum}}\, = \,\overrightarrow A + \overrightarrow B \]

\[\overrightarrow Q = \,{\text{vector}}\,{\text{differences}}\, = \overrightarrow A - \overrightarrow B \]
Since \[\overrightarrow P \]and \[\overrightarrow Q \]are perpendicular to
\[\begin{gathered}
  \overrightarrow P \cdot \overrightarrow Q = 0 \\
   \Rightarrow \,\left( {\overrightarrow A + \overrightarrow B } \right) \cdot \left( {\overrightarrow A - \overrightarrow B } \right) = 0 \\
   \Rightarrow \,{A^2} = {B^2}\,\, \Rightarrow \,|\overrightarrow A |\, = \,|\overrightarrow B | \\
\end{gathered} \]

Question: Number of particles is given by \[n =  - D\frac{{{n_2} - {n_1}}}{{{x_2} - {x_1}}}\] crossing a unit area perpendicular to X-axis in unit time, where n<sub>1</sub> and n<sub>2</sub> are number of particles per unit volume for the value of x meant to be x<sub>2</sub> and x<sub>1</sub> respectively. Find dimensions of D called as diffusion constant
Solution:
(n) = Number of particle passing from unit area in unit time \[ = \frac{{{\text{No}}{\text{.}}\,\,{\text{of}}\,\,{\text{particle}}}}{{A \times t}} = \frac{{\left[ {{M^0}{L^0}{T^0}} \right]}}{{\left[ {{L^2}} \right]\left[ T \right]}} = \left[ {{L^{ - 2}}{T^{ - 1}}} \right]\]

\[\left[ {{n_1}} \right] = \left[ {{n_2}} \right] = \]No. of particle in unit volume \[ = \left[ {{L^{ - 3}}} \right]\]

Now from the given formula \[\left[ D \right] = \frac{{\left[ n \right]\left[ {{x_2} - {x_1}} \right]}}{{\left[ {{n_2} - {n_1}} \right]}} = \frac{{\left[ {{L^{ - 2}}{T^{ - 1}}} \right]\left[ L \right]}}{{\left[ {{L^{ - 3}}} \right]}} = \left[ {{L^2}{T^{ - 1}}} \right].\]

Question: After 280 days, the activity of a radio active sample is 6000 dps.  The activity reduces to 3000 dps after another 140 days.  The initial activity of the sample in dps is
A radioactive nucleus (initial mass number A and atomic number Z emits 3 \[\alpha \] - particles and 2 positrons. The ratio of number of neutrons to that of protons in the final nucleus will be
Solution:
As a result of emission of 1 \[\alpha \] - particle, the mass number decreases by 4 units and atomic number decreases by 2 units. And by the emission of 1 positron the atomic number decreases by 1 unit but mass number remains constant
\[\therefore \] Mass number of final nucleus = A – 12
Atomic number of final nucleus = Z – 8
\[\therefore \] Number of neutrons = (A – 12) – (Z – 8)
= A – Z – 4
Number of protons = Z – 8
\[\therefore \] Required ratio \[ = \frac{{A - Z - 4}}{{Z - 8}}\]

Question: Which kind of life-cycle pattern is exhibited by seed-bearing plants?

This type of life cycle is called the diplontic life cycle which is seen in all the seed-bearing plants (spermatophytes).

Question: The work done in turning a magnet of magnetic moment M by an angle of 90o from the equilibrium position is n times the corresponding work done to turn it through an angle of 60o is
Solution:
$$W = MB\left( {\cos {\theta _1} - \cos {\theta _2}} \right)$$
$$In\,\,first\,\,case,{\theta _1} = {0^o}\,\,and\,\,{\theta _2} = {90^o}$$
$$ \Rightarrow {W_1} = MB\left( {\cos \,{0^o} - \cos {{90}^o}} \right) = MB$$
In Second case $${\theta _1} = {0^o},{\theta _2} = {60^o}$$
$$ \Rightarrow \,{W_2} = MB\left( {\cos {0^o} - \cos {{60}^o}} \right)$$
$${W_2} = MB\left( {1 - \frac{1}{2}} \right) = \frac{{MB}}{2}$$
$$Given,\,\,{W_1} = n{W_2}$$
$$ \Rightarrow MB = n\frac{{MB}}{2}$$
$$ \Rightarrow n = 2$$

Question: The volume occupied by an atom is greater than the volume of the nucleus by factor of about
Solution:
The volume is directly proportional to the cube of the radius of a sphere.
Now, the radius of an atom, ${r_{atom}} = {10^{ - 10}}{\text{ m}}$, and the radius of a nucleus is, ${r_{nucleus}} = {10^{ - 15}}{\text{ m}}$
Therefore, the ratio of the volume of the atom upon the volume of the nucleus is,
$\frac{{{V_{atom}}}}{{{V_{nucleus}}}} = \frac{{\left( {\frac{4}{3}\pi r_{atom}^3} \right)}}{{\left( {\frac{4}{3}\pi r_{nucleus}^3} \right)}} = \frac{{r_{atom}^3}}{{r_{nucleus}^3}}$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{{V_{atom}}}}{{{V_{nucleus}}}} = {\left( {\frac{{{{10}^{ - 10}}}}{{{{10}^{ - 15}}}}} \right)^3}$
$\therefore \frac{{{V_{atom}}}}{{{V_{nucleus}}}} = {10^{15}}$
Therefore, ${V_{atom}} = {V_{nucleus}} \times {10^{15}}{\text{ m}}$

Question: To determine the Young’s modulus of a wire, the formula is \[Y = \frac{F}{A}.\frac{L}{{\Delta l}};\] where L = length, A = area of cross – section of the wire, ΔL = Change in length of the wire when stretched with a force F. The conversion factor to change it from CGS to MKS system is

\[\frac{{{n_2}}}{{{n_1}}} = {\left[ {\frac{{gm}}{{{{10}^3}gm}}} \right]^1}{\left[ {\frac{{cm}}{{{{10}^2}cm}}} \right]^{ - 1}}{\left[ {\frac{{\sec }}{{\sec }}} \right]^{ - 2}} = \frac{1}{{10}} = 0.1\]

Question: In a series of successive measurements in an experiment, the readings of the period of oscillation of a simple pendulum were found to be 2.63 s, 2.56 s, 2.42 s, 2.71 s and 2.80 s. Find the time period of the simple pendulum.
Solution:
True or mean value, Tm

\[{a_m} = \frac{{{a_1} + {a_2} + {a_3} + {a_4} + .... + {a_n}}}{n}\]

\[{T_m} = \frac{{{T_1} + {T_2} + {T_3} + {T_4} + {T_5}}}{5}\]

\[ = \frac{{2.63 + 2.56 + 2.42 + 2.71 + 2.80}}{5}\]

\[{T_m} = 2.62\;s\]

Time periodTime
T12.63 s
T22.56 s
T32.42 s
T42.71 s
T52.80 s

Absolute errors in each measurement,

\[\Delta {T_n} = \left| {{T_n} - {T_m}} \right|\]

\[\Delta {T_1} = \left| {{T_1} - {T_m}} \right| = \left| {2.63 - 2.62} \right| = 0.01\;s\]

\[\Delta {T_2} = \left| {{T_2} - {T_m}} \right| = \left| {2.56 - 2.62} \right| = 0.06\;s\]

\[\Delta {T_3} = \left| {{T_3} - {T_m}} \right| = \left| {2.42 - 2.62} \right| = 0.20\;s\]

\[\Delta {T_4} = \left| {{T_4} - {T_m}} \right| = \left| {2.71 - 2.62} \right| = 0.09\;s\]

\[\Delta {T_5} = \left| {{T_5} - {T_m}} \right| = \left| {2.80 - 2.62} \right| = 0.18\;s\]

Mean absolute error of ΔTm

\[\overline {\Delta {T_m}} = \frac{{\left| {\Delta {T_1}} \right| + \left| {\Delta {T_2}} \right| + \left| {\Delta {T_3}} \right| + \left| {\Delta {T_4}} \right| + \left| {\Delta {T_5}} \right|}}{5}\]

\[\overline {\Delta {T_m}} = \frac{{0.01 + 0.06 + 0.20 + 0.09 + 0.18}}{5}\]

\[ = \frac{{0.54}}{5} = 0.108\;s\]

\[\overline {\Delta {T_m}} = 0.11\;s\]

Percentage error of ΔTm

\[{\text{Percentage}}\,\,{\text{error}} = \frac{{\overline {\Delta {T_m}} }}{{{T_m}}} \times 100 = \frac{{0.11}}{{2.62}} \times 100\]

\[T = 0.042 \times 100 = 4\% \]

Time period of simple pendulum

\[T = 2.62 \pm 4\% \]

Thus, option (A) is the correct answer.

Question: Let $\cos \left( {\alpha  + \beta } \right) = \frac{4}{5}$ and let $\sin \left( {\alpha  - \beta } \right) = \frac{5}{{13}},$ where $0 \leqslant \alpha ,\beta  \leqslant \frac{\pi }{4},$ then $\tan 2\alpha  = $
Solution:
$\cos \left( {\alpha + \beta } \right) = \frac{4}{5} \Rightarrow \tan \left( {\alpha + \beta } \right) = \frac{3}{4}$
$\sin \left( {\alpha - \beta } \right) = \frac{5}{{13}} \Rightarrow \tan \left( {\alpha - \beta } \right) = \frac{5}{{12}}$
$\tan 2\alpha = \tan \left( {\alpha + \beta + \alpha - \beta } \right) = \frac{{\frac{3}{4} + \frac{5}{{12}}}}{{1 - \frac{3}{4}\frac{5}{{12}}}} = \frac{{56}}{{33}}$

Question: If $\overrightarrow A  = 2\widehat i + 3\widehat j + 4\widehat k$ and $\overrightarrow B  = 4\widehat i + 3\widehat j + 2\widehat k,$ then angle between $\overrightarrow A $ and $\overrightarrow B $ is
Solution:
$\overrightarrow A = 2\widehat i + 3\widehat j + 4\widehat k$ and $\overrightarrow B = 4\widehat i + 3\widehat j + 2\widehat k$

Let angle between $\overrightarrow A $ and $\overrightarrow B $ is

$\overrightarrow A \cdot \overrightarrow B = AB\cos \theta $

$ \Rightarrow \,\,\left( {8 + 9 + 8} \right) = \left( {\sqrt {4 + 9 + 16} } \right)$

$\left( {\sqrt {16 + 9 + 4} } \right)\cos \theta $

Question: Two short bar magnets P and Q are arranged such that their centres are on the X-axis and are separated by a large distance. The magnetic axes of P and Q are along X and Y-axes, respectively. At a point R, mid-way between their centres, if B is the magnitude of induction due to Q, the magnitude of total induction at R due to the both magnets is
Solution:
Since for magnet P, axis lies along X – axis and for magnet Q, axis is along Y – axis. The point R is along axial line w.r.t. magnet P and is along equatorial line w.r.t. magnet Q.
Hence, magnetic field due to magnet Q,
$${B_Q} = \frac{{{\mu _0}}}{{4\pi }}\frac{M}{{{x^3}}} = B\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...\left( i \right)$$
Magnetic field due to magnet P,
$${B_P} = \frac{{{\mu _0}}}{{4\pi }}\frac{{2M}}{{{x^3}}} = 2B\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...\left( {ii} \right)$$
As at point R, magnetic field due to P and Q magnet are perpendicular to each other,
$${B_R} \Rightarrow $$ Net magnetic field at R due to magnet P and Q is given as
$${B_R} \Rightarrow \sqrt {B_P^2 + B_Q^2} $$
[From Eqs. (i) and (ii)]
$$ = \sqrt {{B^2} + {{\left( {2B} \right)}^2}} = \sqrt 5 \,B$$

Question: Domain formation is the necessary feature of
Solution: According to Weiss, due to interaction of spin of electrons of one atom with that of neighbouring atoms, a ferromagnetic material gets divided into small regions called domains, each of which is at all times completely magnetised. But the direction of magnetisation varies from domain to domain, so that the resultant magnetisation of the material is zero.

Question: The centripetal force (F) acting on an object that is rotating in a circular path depends upon the mass of object (m), radius of the circular path (r), and velocity (v) of the object. Derive the formula for the centripetal force.
Solution:
Let \[F = k{m^a}{r^b}{v^c}\]

Then,

\[\left[ F \right] = \left[ k \right]\left[ {{m^a}} \right]\left[ {{r^b}} \right]\left[ {{v^c}} \right]\]

\[\left[ {ML{T^{ - 2}}} \right] = \left[ {{M^0}{L^0}{T^0}} \right]\left[ {{M^a}} \right]\left[ {{L^b}} \right]\left[ {{L^c}{T^{ - c}}} \right]\]

\[\left[ {ML{T^{ - 2}}} \right] = \left[ {{M^a}} \right]\left[ {{L^{b + c}}} \right]\left[ {{T^{ - c}}} \right]\]

On comparing, we get

\[a = 1,\,\,c = 2,\,\,b + c = 1 \to b = - 1\]

So,

\[F = km{v^2}{r^{ - 1}}\]

(iii) $D + E \to F + \varepsilon $
(iv) $F \to D + E + \varepsilon $

The reactions for which product is/are more stable than reactance, energy will be positive.

C > A
D > F
And E > F
$\eqalign{
  & \left( {\text{i}} \right)\,\,A + B \to C + \varepsilon \cr
  & \left( {{\text{iv}}} \right)\,F \to D + E + \varepsilon \cr} $
are the reactions for which $\varepsilon $ will be positive. i.e. option a is the correct answer.

41.9 cm3 of nitrogen at S.T.P weight

Question: Given $\vec A = \hat i + 2\hat j - 3\hat k.$ When a vector $\vec B$ is added to $\overrightarrow A $ , we get a unit vector along X–axis. Then, $\vec B$is
Solution:
$\overrightarrow {\text{A}} = \widehat {\text{i}} + 2\widehat {\text{j}} - 3\widehat {\text{k}}$
\[\begin{gathered}
  {\text{Let}}\,\vec B = x\hat i + y\hat j + z\hat k\, \\
  {\text{Given}}\,\overrightarrow A + \overrightarrow B = \widehat i \\
   \Rightarrow (1 + x)\hat i + (2 + y)\hat j + (z - 3)\hat k = 1\hat i \\
  {\text{Since}},x,y\,\,{\text{and}}\,\,z\,{\text{components}}\,{\text{are}}\,{\text{independent}}\,{\text{to}}\,{\text{each}}\,{\text{other}}. \\
  {\text{Equating}}\,{\text{them}}\,{\text{on}}\,{\text{eighter}}\,{\text{side}}.1 + x = 1 \\
   \Rightarrow x = 0 \\
  2 + y = 0 \\
   \Rightarrow y = - 2z - 3 = 0 \\
   \Rightarrow z = 3 \\
   \Rightarrow \vec B = - 2\hat j + 3\hat k \\
\end{gathered} \]

Question: How many significant figures will be there in the calculated solution of following problem: \[\frac{{\left( {29.2 - 20.2} \right) \times \left( {1.97 \times {{10}^5}} \right)}}{{1.39}}\]?
Solution:
Correct option is (D)

The result of addition and subtraction cannot have more digits to the right of the decimal point than either of the original numbers.

In case of division and multiplication, the result must be reported with no more significant figures as are there in the measurement with the few significant figures.

Therefore, 29.2 – 20.2 = 9.0.

Thus, the answer to this subtraction will be upto one decimal place.

Now \[\frac{{\left( {29.2 - 20.2} \right) \times \left( {1.97 \times {{10}^5}} \right)}}{{1.39}} = \frac{{\left( {9.0} \right) \times \left( {1.97 \times {{10}^5}} \right)}}{{1.39}}\]

Thus, the answer to this term will be upto 2 significant figures.

Hence, option D is correct.

Question:
Assertion: Asexual reproduction is more important with regard to biotechnology.
Reason: Asexual reproduction preserves the genetic information while sexual reproduction permits variations
Options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
Solution:
Prokaryotes and eukaryotes reproduce asexually, they transfer a nearly identical copy of their genetic material to their offspring through vertical gene transfer.
The advantages of asexual reproduction over sexual reproduction are:
1) Rapid rate of reproduction: large number of offspring is formed if the environment is favorable; hence they reproduce at a rapid rate. More individuals are formed from a single cell in less time.
2) Better chances of survival: Since large number of offspring are formed, they have better chances of survival.
3) The offspring produced are exactly identical to parents, so they retain the qualities of the parent cell while sexual reproduction permits variation.

Question:
The mass of proton is 1.0073 u and that of neutron is 1.0087 u (u = atomic mass unit) The binding energy of 2He4 is (mass of helium nucleus = 4.0015 u)
Given, mass of helium nucleus 4.0015 u.
Solution:
Mass of proton, ${m_p} = 1.0073{\text{ u}}$, and mass of the neutron, ${m_n} = 1.0087{\text{ u}}$
Mass of the nucleus, $m = 4.0015{\text{ u}}$
Now, the total mass of ${}_2^4He$is,
$\Delta m = \left[ {2\left( {{m_p} + {m_n}} \right) - m} \right]$ because helium has two neutrons and two protons.
Therefore,
$\Delta m = \left[ {2\left( {1.0073 + 1.0087} \right) - 4.0015} \right] = 0.0305$
Now, the binding energy of Helium is,
$BE = 931\Delta m = 931 \times 0.0305$
(where,$1amu = u = 931{\text{ MeV}}$)
$\therefore BE = 28.3955{\text{ MeV}}$

Question: If $A:B = 5:3,$ then what is the value of $\left( {A + B} \right):\left( {A - B} \right)$?
Solution:
According to Componendo and dividendo rule,

If $\left( {\frac{a}{b}} \right) = \left( {\frac{c}{d}} \right),$ then $\frac{{\left( {a + b} \right)}}{{\left( {a - b} \right)}} = \frac{{\left( {c + d} \right)}}{{\left( {c - d} \right)}}$

Hence,

If $A:B = 5:3$

$\therefore \,\,\,\left( {A + B} \right):\left( {A - B} \right) = \left( {5 + 3} \right):\left( {5 - 3} \right) = 8:2 = 4:1$

Question: Recognise the figure and find out the correct matching
Options: a-haplontic life cycle, b-diplontic life cycle, c-haplodiplontic life cycle
a-haplodiplontic life cycle, b-diplontic life cycle, c-haplontic life cycle
a-diplontic life cycle, b-haplodiplontic life cycle, c-haplontic life cycle
a-haplontic life cycle, b-haplodiplontic life cycle, c-diplontic life cycle,
Solution:
-The life cycle with the dominant haploid gametophyte generation is called the haplontic life cycle.
-The life cycle with dominant diploid sporophytic generation is called the diplontic life cycle.

Solution:

 $ \frac{1}{2}m{v^2} + \frac{{K\left( {2e} \right)\left( {Ze} \right)}}{\infty } $ $ = 0 + \frac{{K\left( {2e} \right)\left( {Ze} \right)}}{r} $
 $ \frac{1}{2}m{v^2} = \frac{{K\left( {2e} \right)\left( {Ze} \right)}}{r} $
 $ r = \frac{{4kZ{e^2}}}{{m{v^2}}} $
 $ r \propto \frac{1}{m} $

Question: <sub>6</sub>C<sup>12</sup> absorbs an energetic neutron and emits a beta particle. The resulting nucleus is
The greater BOD of wastewater
Options: Relates the decrease of temperature of water
Solution: The greater BOD of wastewater decreases the oxygen content of water. Biochemical oxygen demand (BOD) is an amount of dissolved oxygen that would be consumed if all the organic matter in one liter of water was oxidized by microorganisms (at 20°C). It is an indicator of water quality. If the value of BOD is high then water is highly polluted by organic matter. If the value of BOD is low then water is either normal or less polluted by organic matter.

\[{E_0} = C{B_0}\]
\[C = \frac{1}{{\sqrt {{\mu _0}{\varepsilon _0}} }}\]
Substituting the values,

Question: The component of vector \[\vec A = {a_x}\hat i + {a_y}\hat j + {a_z}\hat k\] along the direction of \[\left( {\hat i - \hat j} \right)\] is
Solution:
\[\overrightarrow A = {a_x}\widehat i + {a_y}\widehat j + {a_z}\widehat k,\overrightarrow B = \widehat i - \widehat j\]

Component of \[\overrightarrow A \] along \[\overrightarrow B = A\cos \theta = \overrightarrow A \cdot \left( {\widehat B} \right)\]

\[ = \left( {{a_x}\widehat i + {a_y}\widehat j + {a_z}\widehat k} \right) \cdot \frac{{\left( {\widehat i - \widehat j} \right)}}{{\sqrt 2 }} = \frac{{\left( {{a_x} - {a_y}} \right)}}{{\sqrt 2 }}\]

Question: When light of wavelength 300 nm (nanometer) falls on a photoelectric emitter, photoelectrons are liberated. For another emitter, however, light of 600 nm wavelength is sufficient for creating photoemission, what is the ratio of the work functions of the two emitters?
Solution:
The minimum energy required to release an electron from a metal surface, work function, $\phi = \frac{{hc}}{\lambda }$
Now, the ratio of work function for two emitters,
$\frac{{{\phi _1}}}{{{\phi _2}}} = \frac{{{\lambda _2}}}{{{\lambda _1}}}$
Where, the wavelength of the first emitter is,
λ1 = 300 nm, and the second emitter,
λ2 = 600 nm
Therefore, $ \Rightarrow \frac{{{\phi _1}}}{{{\phi _2}}} = \frac{{600}}{{300}}$
$\therefore \frac{{{\phi _1}}}{{{\phi _2}}} = 2$

Question: The binding energy per nucleon is maximum in case of
Solution:
The net binding energy of a nucleus is the difference between the nuclear attraction and the electrostatic repulsion between positively charged protons. As the atomic number of an element
increases its binding energy also increases but proton-proton repulsion also increases.
Therefore, the number of protons is less in iron $_{26}F{e^{56}}$, so it is more stable and has maximum
binding energy per nucleon, and has less proton-proton repulsion.

Question: m<sub>p</sub> and m<sub>n</sub> are masses of proton and neutron respectively. An element of mass m has Z protons and N neutrons, then
Options: m may be greater than, less than or equal to Zm<sub>p</sub> + Nm<sub>n</sub>, depending on nature of element
Solution:
Mass of proton and neutrons are ${m_p}$ and ${m_n}$,
respectively. m is the mass of an element. Z and N are protons and neutrons of an element.
Now, the atomic mass of any stable nuclei is always less than the sum of the masses of the constituent particles.
Therefore, $m < \left[ {N{m_n} + Z{m_p}} \right]$

Question: Which of the following organisms do not reproduce?
Solution: A mule is a hybrid of a male donkey and a female horse. They are sexually sterile. Honeybees are social polymorphic insects. Only the queen in honey bees reproduce. Human beings who are infertile are also not able to reproduce.

Question: The value of λ for which the vectors \[3\widehat i - 6\widehat j + \widehat k\] and \[2\widehat i - 4\widehat j + \lambda \widehat k\] are parallel is
Solution:
Let

$\overrightarrow a = 3\widehat i - 6\widehat j + \widehat k$

$\overrightarrow b = 2\widehat i - 4\widehat j + \lambda \widehat k$

Since the given vectors are parallel,

∴ Angle between them is 0°

So $\overrightarrow a \cdot \overrightarrow b = \left| {\overrightarrow a } \right|\left| {\overrightarrow b } \right|\cos 0$

\[ \Rightarrow \,\,\left( {3\widehat i - 6\widehat j + \widehat k} \right) \cdot \left( {2\widehat i - 4\widehat j + \lambda \widehat k} \right)\]

\[ = \left| {3\widehat i - 6\widehat j + \widehat k} \right|\left| {2\widehat i - 4\widehat j + \lambda \widehat k} \right|\]

$6 + 24 + \lambda = \sqrt {9 + 36 + 1} \cdot \sqrt {4 + 16 + {\lambda ^2}} $

$30 + \lambda = \sqrt {46} \cdot \sqrt {20 + {\lambda ^2}} $

Squaring both sides, we get

$900 + {\lambda ^2} + 60\lambda = 46\left( {20 + {\lambda ^2}} \right)$

$ \Rightarrow \,\,900 + {\lambda ^2} + 60\lambda = 920 + 46{\lambda ^2}$

$ \Rightarrow \,\,{\lambda ^2} - 46{\lambda ^2} + 60\lambda + 900 - 920 = 0$

$ \Rightarrow \,\, - 45{\lambda ^2} + 60\lambda - 20 = 0$

$ \Rightarrow \,\,9{\lambda ^2} - 12\lambda + 4 = 0$

$ \Rightarrow \,\,{\left( {3\lambda - 2} \right)^2} = 0$

$ \Rightarrow \,\,3\lambda - 2 = 0$

$ \Rightarrow \,\,3\lambda = 2$

$\therefore \,\,\,\lambda = \frac{2}{3}$

Alternate method:

Let $\overrightarrow a = {a_1}\widehat i + {a_2}\widehat j + {a_3}\widehat k$ and $\overrightarrow b = {b_1}\widehat i + {b_2}\widehat j + {b_3}\widehat k$

If $\overrightarrow a \parallel \overrightarrow b $

$\therefore \,\,\,\frac{{{a_1}}}{{{b_1}}} = \frac{{{a_2}}}{{{b_2}}} = \frac{{{a_3}}}{{{b_3}}}$

$ \Rightarrow \,\,\frac{3}{2} = \frac{{ - 6}}{{ - 4}} = \frac{1}{\lambda }\,\,\, \Rightarrow \,\,\frac{1}{\lambda } = \frac{3}{2}\,\,\, \Rightarrow \,\,\lambda = \frac{2}{3}$

Question: The magnitude of the X and Y components of \[\vec A\] are 7 and 6. Also the magnitudes of X and Y components of \[\vec A + \vec B\] are 11 and 9 respectively. What is the magnitude of \[\overrightarrow B \]?
Solution:
\[\overrightarrow A = 7\widehat i + 6\widehat j\]

Let, \[\overrightarrow B = x\widehat i + y\widehat j + z\hat k\]

\[\overrightarrow A + \overrightarrow B = 11\widehat i + 9\widehat j\]

\[ \Rightarrow \left( {7 + x} \right)\widehat i + \left( {6 + y} \right)\widehat j + z\hat k = 11\widehat i + 9\widehat j\]

Equating Components

\[7 + x = 11 \Rightarrow x = 4\]

\[6 + y = 9 \Rightarrow y = 3\]

\[z = 0 \Rightarrow z = 0\]

\[ \Rightarrow \overrightarrow B = 4\widehat i + 3\widehat j\]

\[\therefore \,\,\,B = \sqrt {{4^2} + {3^2}} = \,5\;{\text{units}}\]

Question: M<sub>p</sub> denotes the mass of a proton and M<sub>n</sub> that of a neutron. A given nucleus, of binding energy B, contains Z protons and N neutrons. The mass M(N, Z) of the nucleus is given by (c is the velocity of light)
Solution:
We know that
E = mc2 (Einstein’s mass energy equation)
If we have N neutrons and Z protons and we wish to create an atom then for the nucleus to be stable, the same energy is taken out from it, which is known binding energy. Basically.
Binding Energy = ${{\text{M}}_{{\text{theoritica}}{{\text{l}}^{{{\text{C}}^{\text{2}}}}}}}{\text{ - }}{{\text{M}}_{{\text{practica}}{{\text{l}}^{{{\text{C}}^{\text{2}}}}}}}$
${{\text{M}}_{{\text{practica}}{{\text{l}}^{{{\text{C}}^{\text{2}}}}}}}{\text{ = }}\,{{\text{M}}_{{\text{theoritica}}{{\text{l}}^{{{\text{C}}^{\text{2}}}}}}}{\text{ - }}\,{\text{B}}{\text{.E}}$
${{\text{M}}_{{\text{practical}}}}{\text{ = }}\,{{\text{M}}_{{\text{theoritical}}}}\,{\text{ - }}\,\,\frac{{{\text{B}}{\text{.E}}}}{{{{\text{c}}^{\text{2}}}}}$
${\text{M}}\left( {{\text{N,Z}}} \right){\text{ = }}\left[ {{{\text{M}}_{{\text{protons}}}}{\text{ + }}{{\text{M}}_{{\text{neutrons}}}}} \right]{\text{ - }}\frac{{{\text{B}}{\text{.E}}}}{{{{\text{c}}^{\text{2}}}}}$
$ \Rightarrow M\left( {N,Z} \right) = Z{M_p} + N{M_n} - \frac{B}{{{c^2}}}$

Question: The density of a cube is measured by measuring its mass and length of its sides. If the maximum error in the measurement of mass and length are 4% and 3% respectively, the maximum error in the measurement of density will be
Solution:
\[{\text{Density}} = \frac{{{\text{Mass}}}}{{{\text{Volume}}}}\]

\[\rho = \frac{M}{{{L^3}}}\,\,\,\therefore \,\,\,\frac{{\Delta \rho }}{\rho } = \frac{{\Delta M}}{M} + 3\frac{{\Delta L}}{L}\]

% error in density = % error in Mass + 3 (% error in length)

\[ = 4 + 3\left( 3 \right) = 13\% \]

Solution: Chemical fertilizers, applied to crop plants, pollute both the soil as well as water, when they are carried through runoff due to heavy rains or excessive irrigation of the fields and finally reach into the water bodies like lakes, rivers, etc.

As \[R = {R_0}{A^{1/3}}\]\[\left( {{R_0} = 1.1 \times {{10}^{-15}}m} \right)\]

Question: A ground-state electron (in a hydrogen atom) absorb a photon of energy 3E<sub>0</sub>. How much kinetic energy does the electron now have? (\[{{\text{E}}_0}\]is the energy in the ground state of electron)
Solution:
Energy of electron in ground state = \[ - {{\text{E}}_0}\]
So total final energy = \[ - {{\text{E}}_0} + 3{{\text{E}}_0} = 2{{\text{E}}_0}\]

Solution:
Curie is the unit to measure the intensity of the radioactivity. There are three units to measure radioactivity or radioactive decay; dps (disintegration per second) and Becquerel $\left( {Bq} \right)$.
Now, one curie, $1{\text{ Ci}} = 3.7 \times {10^{10}}{\text{ dps}}$

Question: <sup>22</sup>Ne nucleus, after absorbing energy, decays into two α-particles and an unknown nucleus. The unknown nucleus is
Solution:
The nuclear reaction is;
${}_{10}^{22}Ne \to {}_2^4He + {}_2^4He + {}_6^{14}X$
The new element X is Carbon, ${}_6^{14}C$
The emission of two alpha-particle reduces the atomic number by 4 units and the mass number by 8 units.

$ = {P_x}{Q_x} + {P_y}{Q_y} + {P_z}{Q_z}$

$ = 6 + 20$

$ = 26$

Question: Number of ejected photoelectron increases with increase
Solution: The number of ejected photoelectrons increases with an increase in the intensity of light, because the photoelectric current is directly proportional to the intensity of the incident light. The intensity of incident light is equal to the energy per unit time per unit area. Hence, keeping the frequency constant and increasing the intensity of incident light will increase the number of ejected electrons.
