Question: The wavelength associated with an electron, accelerated through a potential difference of 100 V, is of the order of
Solution:
The de-Broglie wavelength associated with an electron, $\lambda = \frac{h}{p} = \frac{h}{{\sqrt {2meV} }}$
Where, potential difference, $V = 100{\text{ V}}$, and mass of the electron, $m = 9.1 \times {10^{ - 31}}{\text{ kg}}$
$ \Rightarrow \lambda = \frac{{6.63 \times {{10}^{ - 34}}}}{{\sqrt {2 \times 9.1 \times {{10}^{ - 31}} \times 1.6 \times {{10}^{ - 19}} \times 100} }}$
\[\therefore \lambda = 1.2 \times {10^{ - 10}}{\text{ }}m = 1.2\mathop A\limits^o \]

Question: The work function of metal is 1 eV. Light of wavelength 3000 Å is incident on this metal surface. The velocity of emitted photo-electrons will be
Solution:
$${hv = W + \frac{1}{2}m{v^2}}$$

$${\frac{{hc}}{\lambda } = W + \frac{1}{2}m{v^2}}$$

$${{\text{Here }}\lambda  = 3000\mathop {\text{A}}\limits^ \circ  }$$

$${ = 3000 \times {{10}^{ - 10}}\;{\text{m}}}$$

$${W = 1{\text{eV}} = 1.6 \times {{10}^{ - 19}}{\text{joule}}}$$

$${\frac{{\left( {6.6 \times {{10}^{ - 34}}} \right)\left( {3 \times {{10}^8}} \right)}}{{3000 \times {{10}^{ - 10}}}}}$$

$$ = \left( {1.6 \times {{10}^{ - 19}}} \right) + \frac{1}{2} \times \left( {9.1 \times {{10}^{ - 31}}} \right){v^2}$$

Solving we get $$v \cong {10^6}\;{\text{m}}/{\text{s}}$$

Question: Relative permeability of iron is 5500, then its magnetic susceptibility will be

Question: The net force between two nucleons 1 fm apart is F<sub>1</sub> if both are protons, F<sub>2</sub> if both are neutrons, and F<sub>3</sub> if one is a neutron and the other is a proton.
Solution:
Nuclear force is a very short range force and it is independent of the charge on the nucleons. So the attractive nuclear force is same for any pair of nucleons.
The electrostatics force is a long range force and it acts only between charge particles i.e. protons and protons will repel each other.
So force between protons is Repulsive (electrostatic) as well as attractive (Nuclear) while that between 2 neutrons and between 1 neutron and 1 Proton is only nuclear(attractive).
So, ${{\text{F}}_{\text{1}}}\,{\text{ < }}\,\,{{\text{F}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{ = }}{{\text{F}}_{\text{3}}}$

Question: The momentum of a photon is $3.3 \times {10^{ - 29}}kg - {\text{m}}\,{{\text{s}}^{ - 1}}$. Its frequency will be
Solution:
Momentum, $p = \frac{E}{c} = \frac{{hv}}{c}$
$\therefore \,v = \frac{{pc}}{h} = \frac{{3.3 \times {{10}^{ - 29}} \times 3 \times {{10}^8}}}{{6.6 \times {{10}^{ - 34}}}}$
$ = 1.5 \times {10^{13}}Hz$

To obtain a virus-free plant, which of the following can be used as an explant in micropropagation?
Solution: Explants are small plant parts or tissues that are aseptically cut and used to initiate a culture in a nutrient medium. Generally, plant shoot apical meristems or axillary meristems are virus-free. This is because the spread of the virus in the plant tissue is slow whereas meristem shows rapid growth if it is actively producing new leaves/flowers. The meristem grows into a virus-free shoot that is rooted to get a virus-free plant.

Question: The largest mass (m) that can be moved by a flowing river depends on velocity (v), density $\left( \rho  \right)$ of river water and acceleration due to gravity (g). The correct relation is

Question: The cathode of a photoelectric cell is changed such that the work function changes from W<sub>1</sub> to W<sub>2</sub> (W<sub>2</sub>> W<sub>1</sub>). If the current before and after changes are I<sub>1</sub> and I<sub>2</sub>, all other conditions remaining unchanged, then (assuming hν > W<sub>2</sub>)
Solution:
According to the question, work function \[{W_2} > {W_1}\].
Since hν > W2 photo-electrons will be emitted
Now, the photoelectric current is independent of the work function.
The photoelectric current is directly proportional to the intensity of incident rays. Since, there is no
change in the intensity of incident rays, ${I_1} = {I_2}$.
Therefore, the current will be the same after changing the value of the work function

Question:
Which of the following diseases is caused due to SO2?
I. Digestive diseases

II. Asthma

III. Bronchitis

IV. Emphysema
Solution: It has been reported that even a low concentration of sulphur dioxide causes respiratory diseases. e.g., asthma bronchitis, emphysema in human beings. SO<sub>2</sub> causes irritation to the eyes, resulting in tears and redness. High concentration of SO<sub>2</sub> leads to stiffness of flower buds. Which eventually fall off from plants.

Question: If EA, EB and EC represents kinetic energies of an electron, alpha particle and proton respectively and each moving with same de-Broglie wavelength then choose the correct among the following.
Solution:
As we know,
\[\lambda = \frac{h}{{mv}} = \frac{h}{{\sqrt {2mKE} }}\]

For same value of wavelength,
\[\because \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,K.E. \propto \frac{1}{{\sqrt m }}\]
Mass of \[e,\,\,\alpha \] and \[p\]are
\[{m_e} < {m_p} < {m_\alpha }\]

The correct order is

\[{E_e} > {E_p} > {E_\alpha }\]
Hence, \[{E_A} > {E_C} > {E_B}\]

Question: The decay constant of a radioactive sample is λ. The half-life and mean-life of the sample are (respectively) given by:
Solution:
The number of radioactive nuclei is given by,
$N = {N_0}{e^{ - \lambda t}}$
Let after time$t = {t_{\frac{1}{2}}}$the number of nuclei becomes half $N = \frac{{{N_0}}}{2}$
Now, on solving ${t_{\frac{1}{2}}} = \frac{{\ln 2}}{\lambda }$
Now, the mean life of the sample is given by, ${t_{avg}} = \frac{1}{\lambda }$

Question: If $\frac{{\left( {6x + 4y} \right)}}{{\left( {6x - 4y} \right)}} = \frac{8}{6}$ then what is the value of $\frac{{{x^2}}}{{{y^2}}}$?
Solution:
Given:

The expression $\frac{{\left( {6x + 4y} \right)}}{{\left( {6x - 4y} \right)}} = \frac{8}{6}$

Calculation

The expression $\frac{{\left( {6x + 4y} \right)}}{{\left( {6x - 4y} \right)}} = \frac{8}{6}$

Cross multiply,

$36x + 24y = 48x - 32y$

$24y + 32y = 12x$

$56y = 12x$

$\frac{x}{y} = \frac{{56}}{{12}}$

$\frac{x}{y} = \frac{{14}}{3}$

Then \[\frac{{{x^2}}}{{{y^2}}} = \frac{{{{\left( {14} \right)}^2}}}{{{{\left( 3 \right)}^2}}} = \frac{{196}}{9}\]

Therefore, the value of $\frac{{{x^2}}}{{{y^2}}}$ is $\frac{{196}}{9}.$

Question: The nuclei of which one of the following pairs of nuclei are isotones?
Solution:
The nuclei which have the same number of neutrons but the different atomic number and mass number are called Isotones.
Now, according to question;
${}_{34}^{74}Se \Leftrightarrow N = A - Z = 74 - 34 = 40,$
${}_{31}^{71}Ga \Leftrightarrow N = 71 - 31 = 40$
${}_{42}^{92}Mo \Leftrightarrow N = 92 - 42 = 50,$
${}_{40}^{92}Zr \Leftrightarrow N = 92 - 40 = 52$
${}_{38}^{84}Sr \Leftrightarrow N = 84 - 38 = 46,$
${}_{38}^{86}Sr \Leftrightarrow N = 86 - 38 = 48$
${}_{20}^{40}Ca \Leftrightarrow N = 40 - 20 = 20,$
${}_{16}^{32}S \Leftrightarrow N = 32 - 16 = 16$
Therefore, ${}_{34}^{74}Se,{}_{31}^{71}Ga$ have the same number of neutrons, so this pair of nuclei is Isotones.

Question: What is the unit vector along \[\hat i + \hat j\] ?

Unit vector, \[\hat a = \frac{{\overrightarrow a }}{{\left| {\overrightarrow a } \right|}}\]

Here, \[\overrightarrow a = \widehat i + \widehat j\]

\[\therefore \left| {\overrightarrow a } \right| = \sqrt {1 + 1} = \sqrt 2 .\]

\[ \Rightarrow \overrightarrow a = \frac{{\widehat i + \widehat j}}{{\sqrt 2 }}\]

Question: If $\frac{{\sqrt 5  + x}}{{\sqrt 5  - x}} = 5.$ Find the value of x.
Solution:
$\frac{{\sqrt 5 + x}}{{\sqrt 5 - x}} = 5$

Applying Componendo and Dividendo

$\frac{{\sqrt 5 + x + \left( {\sqrt 5 - x} \right)}}{{\left( {\sqrt 5 + x} \right) - \sqrt 5 - x}} = \frac{{5 + 1}}{{5 + 1}}\,\,\, \Rightarrow \,\,\frac{{\sqrt 5 }}{x} = \frac{6}{4}\,\,\, \Rightarrow \,\,x = \frac{{2\sqrt 5 }}{3}$

Question: If $\vec A$ and $\vec B$ are two vectors such that \[\left| {\vec A + \vec B} \right| = \left| {\vec A - \vec B} \right|\] the angle between vectors $\vec A$ and $\vec B$ is :
Solution:
We have
$\left| {\vec A + \vec B} \right| = \left| {\vec A - \vec B} \right|$
$ \Rightarrow {\left| {\vec A + \vec B} \right|^2} = {\left| {\vec A - \vec B} \right|^2}$
$ \Rightarrow {\left| {\vec A} \right|^2} + {\left| {\vec B} \right|^2} + 2\vec A.\vec B = {\left| {\vec A} \right|^2} + {\left| {\vec B} \right|^2} - 2\vec A\vec B$
$ \Rightarrow \vec A.\vec B = 0$
$\therefore \vec A \bot \vec B$
$\therefore \theta = 90^\circ $
Hence, angle between two vectors is $90^\circ $.

Question: If the time period (T) of vibration of a liquid drop depends on surface tension (S), radius (r) of the drop and density (ρ) of the liquid, then the expression of T is
Solution:
Let \[T \propto {S^x}{r^y}{\rho ^z}\] or \[T = K{S^x}{r^y}{\rho ^z}\]

By substituting the dimension of each quantity in both sides

\[\left[ {{M^0}{L^0}{T^1}} \right] = K{\left[ {M{T^{ - 2}}} \right]^x}{\left[ L \right]^y}{\left[ {M{L^{ - 3}}} \right]^z}\]

\[ = \left[ {{M^{x + z}}{L^{y - 3z}}{T^{ - 2x}}} \right]\]

By equating the power of M, L and T in both sides \[x + z = 0,\,\,y - 3z = 0,\,\, - 2x = 1\]

By solving above three equations

\[\therefore \,\,\,x = \frac{{ - 1}}{2},\,\,y = \frac{3}{2},\,\,z = \frac{1}{2}\]

So the time period can be given as,

\[T = K{S^{\frac{{ - 1}}{2}}}{r^{\frac{3}{2}}}{\rho ^{\frac{1}{2}}} = K\sqrt {\frac{{\rho {r^3}}}{S}} .\]

Question: If the binding energy per nucleon in <sub>3</sub>Li<sup>7</sup> and <sub>2</sub>He<sup>4</sup> nuclei are respectively 5.60 MeV and 7.06 MeV, then the energy of proton in the reaction ${}_3^7Li + p \to 2He_2^4$ is
Solution:
The binding energy of Lithium is $5.6{\text{ MeV}}$, and Helium is $7.06{\text{ MeV}}$.
The number of nucleons in Lithium is $7$, and Helium is $4$.
The energy of proton in this reaction ${}_3^7Li + p \to 2He_2^4$ is
Therefore, proton energy $ = $ Binding energy of
Helium atom$ - $Binding energy of Lithium atom
$ \Rightarrow {E_p} = \left( {2 \times 4 \times 7.06} \right) - \left( {7 \times 5.6} \right)$
$\therefore {E_p} = 17.28{\text{ MeV}}$

Question: The susceptibility and permeability of a perfectly diamagnetic substance is
Solution:

and μr = 1 + χm = 1 – 1 = 0
∴ μ = μ0μr = 0.

Question: If \[\left[ {\frac{{\sqrt {\left( {5x + 3} \right)}  + \sqrt {\left( {5x - 3} \right)} }}{{\sqrt {\left( {5x + 3} \right)}  - \sqrt {\left( {5x - 3} \right)} }}} \right] = \frac{5}{3},\] then find the value of 25x.
Solution:
\[\left[ {\frac{{\sqrt {\left( {5x + 3} \right)} + \sqrt {\left( {5x - 3} \right)} }}{{\sqrt {\left( {5x + 3} \right)} - \sqrt {\left( {5x - 3} \right)} }}} \right] = \frac{5}{3}\]

Applying componendo and dividendo we get

\[\frac{{\left\{ {2\sqrt {\left( {5x + 3} \right)} } \right\}}}{{\left\{ {2\sqrt {\left( {5x - 3} \right)} } \right\}}} = \frac{{\left( {5 + 3} \right)}}{{\left( {5 - 3} \right)}}\]

$ \Rightarrow \,\,\frac{{\sqrt {\left( {5x + 3} \right)} }}{{\sqrt {\left( {5x - 3} \right)} }} = \frac{8}{2} = 4$

Squaring both side we get

$\frac{{\left( {5x + 3} \right)}}{{\left( {5x - 3} \right)}} = 16$

Again applying componendo and dividendo we get

$\frac{{10x}}{6} = \frac{{17}}{{15}}$

$ \Rightarrow \,\,x = \frac{{17}}{{25}}$

$\therefore \,\,\,25x = 17$

Question: The resultant of two forces at right angle is 5 N. When the angle between them is 120°, the resultant is \[\sqrt {13} .\] Then the forces are
Solution:
\[{R^2} = {A^2} + {B^2} + 2AB\cos \theta \]

\[{5^2} = {A^2} + {B^2} + 2AB\cos 90^\circ \]

\[{A^2} + {B^2} = 25\]

\[{\left( {\sqrt {13} } \right)^2} = {A^2} + {B^2} + 2AB\cos 120^\circ \]

\[13 = 25 - AB\]

\[AB = 12\]

\[{A^2} + {\left( {\frac{{12}}{A}} \right)^2} = 25\]

\[{A^2} + \frac{{144}}{{{A^2}}} = 25\]

\[{A^4} - 25{A^2} + 144 = 0\]

\[A = 3N\]

\[B = 4N\]

Question: An alpha particle of energy 5 MeV is scattered through $180^\circ $ by a fixed uranium nucleus. The distance of closest approach is of the order of
Solution:
From conservation of mechanical energy
decrease in kinetic energy = increase in potential energy
or $\begin{gathered}
  \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\frac{{\left( {Ze} \right)\left( {2e} \right)}}{{{r_{\min }}}} = 5\,MeV \\
   = 5 \times 1.6 \times {10^{ - 13}}J \\
  \therefore \,{r_{\min }} = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\frac{{2Z{e^2}}}{{5 \times 1.6 \times {{10}^{ - 13}}}} \\
\end{gathered} $
$ = \frac{{\left( {9{\kern 1pt} \times {{10}^9}} \right)\left( 2 \right)\left( {92} \right){{\left( {1.6 \times {{10}^{ - 19}}} \right)}^2}}}{{5 \times 1.6 \times {{10}^{ - 13}}}}$

$\begin{gathered}
   = 5.3 \times {10^{ - 14}}m \\
   = 5.3 \times {10^{ - 12}}cm \\
\end{gathered} $
i.e. rmin is of the order of 10-12 cm.
$\therefore \,$ Correct option is (c).

Question: According to Einstein’s photoelectric equation, the graph of K.E of the photoelectron emitted from the metal versus the frequency of the incident radiation gives a straight-line graph, whose slope
Options: Depends on the intensity of the incident radiation.
Depends on the nature of the metal and also on the intensity of incident radiation.
Is same for all metals and independent of the intensity of the incident radiation.
Depends on the nature of the metal.
Solution:
Kinetic energy of photo electron emitted K. E = hv – φ where v is the frequency of incident radiation and φ is the work function of the metal.

Thus graph of K. E Vs v is a straight line with slope equal to h, having an intercept on negative y-axis which is equal to the work function (φ) of metal. As h is a constant, thus the slope is same for all metals and independent of the intensity of the incident radiation.

Question: The unit vector perpendicular to $\widehat i - 2\widehat j + \widehat k$ and $3\widehat i + \widehat j - 2\widehat k$ is
Solution:
$\overrightarrow A \times \overrightarrow B $ is a vector ⊥ to both $\overrightarrow A $ and $\overrightarrow B $

Now, \[\overrightarrow A \times \overrightarrow B = \left( {\widehat i - 2\widehat j + \widehat k} \right) \times \left( {3\widehat i + \widehat j - 2\widehat k} \right) = 3\widehat i + 5\widehat j + 7\widehat k\]

Now, \[\overrightarrow B = \frac{{\overrightarrow A \times \overrightarrow B }}{{\left| {\overrightarrow A \times \overrightarrow B } \right|}}\]

\[ = \frac{{3\widehat i + 5\widehat j + 7\widehat k}}{{\sqrt {{3^2} + {5^2} + {7^2}} }} = \frac{{3\widehat i + 5\widehat j + 7\widehat k}}{{\sqrt {83} }}\]

Hence, (b) is correct.

Question: How will you purify a liquid having non-volatile impurities?
Solution: Simple distillation will give us the pure liquid while the non-volatile impurities will remain in the flask as residue. For example, sea water containing non-volatile impurities of chlorides and sulphates of Na, K, Ca and Mg can be purified by simple distillation.

Question: Which of the following is positively charged?
Solution:
Alpha particle is a positively charged particle, it has two protons and two neutrons but have no electron to balance the positive charge of the nucleus. On the other hand, the beta particle may be a positively charged or negatively charged particle having magnitude of charge equal to that of electron.
$\gamma - $rays is a neutral particle and it cannot be deflected by the magnetic field but X-rays is an electromagnetic wave of wavelength less than $10{\text{ m}}$.

Question: If \[\overrightarrow A  \cdot \overrightarrow B  = AB,\] then the angle between \[\vec A\,\,{\text{and}}\,\,\vec B\] is
Solution:
\[\overrightarrow A \cdot \overrightarrow B = AB\cos \theta \]

(θ = angle between A and B)

\[ \Rightarrow AB\,\cos \theta = AB \Rightarrow \cos \theta = 1\]

\[ \Rightarrow \theta = 0^\circ \]

Question: Reproduction cannot be
Options: Synonymous of growth in multicellular organisms
Solution: Reproduction and growth in a multicellular organism are mutually exclusive. After a certain time of growth, multicellular organisms start to reproduce. So, they are not synonymous.

Question: The total energy of electron in the ground state of hydrogen atom is –13.6 eV. The kinetic energy of an electron in the first excited state is
Solution:
The total energy of electron of a Hydrogen atom at ground state, $E = - 13.6{\text{ eV}}$
Now, the energy of an electron at first excited state, ${E_1} = - \frac{{13.6}}{{{n^2}}}$
For the first excited state, $n = 2$, then ${E_1} = - 3.4{\text{ eV}}$
Therefore, the kinetic energy of an electron in the first excited state, $K.E = \left| {{E_1}} \right| = 3.4{\text{ eV}}$

Question: In the electromagnetic spectrum, the visible spectrum lies between
Solution: As the range adjacent to the visible spectrum is called the near infrared and the longer wavelength part is called far infrared The region just below the visible in wavelength is called the near ultraviolet. Hence the visible part of electromagnetic spectrum lies in between ultraviolet and infrared.

Question: Lassaigne’s test is not shown by diazonium salts. Why?
Options: Diazonium salts usually lose N<sub>2</sub> on heating
Diazonium salts usually does not lose N2 on heating
Diazonium salts react with fused sodium metal

Question: The hysteresis curve is studied generally for
Solution: Paramagnetic, ferromagnetic, diamagnetic substances are the magnetic materials. These materials are used to make the electro magnets. These substances consist of the different domains if the magnetic materials are kept in the external magnetic field then domains get aligned then magnetization occurs if the external magnetic field is removed then demagnetization occurs. Paramagnetic substance is weakly magnetizing material and ferromagnetic substance is strongly magnetizing material and magnetic substance repels. In strong magnetic material both magnetization and demagnetization occur. So, hysteresis curve is generally studied in the case of the ferromagnetic substance.

Question: The ratio of the areas within the electron orbits for the first excited state to the ground state for the hydrogen atom is:
Solution:
First excited state implies $ n=2 $ , while ground state implies $ n=1 $ . If radius of orbit in ground state, $ {{r}_{1}}={{a}_{0}}, $ then radius of orbit in first excited state
 $ \,={{r}_{2}}={{n}^{2}}{{r}_{1}}={{\left( 2 \right)}^{2}}{{r}_{1}}=4{{r}_{1}}=4{{a}_{0}} $
 $ \therefore \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{{A}_{1}}=\pi {{r}_{1}}^{2}=\pi {{a}_{0}}^{2} $
 $ \text{and}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{{A}_{2}}=\pi {{r}_{2}}^{2}=\pi {{\left( 4{{a}_{0}} \right)}^{2}}=16\pi {{a}_{0}}^{2} $
 $ \text{Hence}\,\,\,\,\,\left( \frac{{{A}_{2}}}{{{A}_{1}}} \right)=\left( \frac{16}{1} \right) $

Tags.Topic: Bohr Model of the Hydrogen Atom

Question: Torques τ<sub>1 </sub>and<sub> </sub>τ<sub>2</sub> are required for magnetic needle to remain perpendicular to the magnetic fields B<sub>1</sub> and B<sub>2</sub> at two different places. The ratio B<sub>1</sub>/B<sub>2</sub> is
The radius of germanium (Ge) nuclide is measured to be twice the radius of \[_4^9Be\]. The number of nucleons in Ge are
Solution:
The radius of the nucleus is, $R \propto {A^{\frac{1}{3}}}$
Let, the radius of the germanium nucleus be $2r$, and ${}_4^9Be$the nucleus be $r$.
Therefore, $\frac{{{R_1}}}{{{R_2}}} = {\left( {\frac{{{A_1}}}{{{A_2}}}} \right)^{\frac{1}{3}}}$where, $A$ is the atomic number
$\frac{r}{{2r}} = {\left( {\frac{9}{{{A_2}}}} \right)^{\frac{1}{3}}}$
Therefore, the number of nucleons in Germanium nuclei is, $\therefore {A_2} = 72$

Question: The angle of dip at a place on the earth gives
Options: The horizontal component of the earth’s magnetic field
the vertical component of the earth’s field
the direction of the earth’s magnetic field
Solution: The dip at a place is defined as the angle made by the direction of the earth’s total magnetic field with horizontal direction. So, the angle of dip at a place on the earth gives the direction of the earth’s magnetic field.

Question: CO emitted automobiles prevents O2-transport to body tissues by:

Obstructing the reaction of O<sub>2</sub> with haemoglobin
Solution: CO emitted by automobiles prevents O<sub>2</sub> transport to body tissue by forming a stable compound with haemoglobin.

Question: A mixture of acetone and CCl<sub>4</sub> can be separated by
Solution:
1. Azeotropic distillation is used only to seperate azeotropes ie. constant boiling mixture. Here acetone & CCl4 are not azeotropic mixture. Hence (a) is not correct.
2. CCl4 & acetone differ in their boiling Point. So it can be seperated through fractional distillation. Hence (b) is correct.
3. Steam distillation is used only in case of steam volatile substance. Hence (c) is not correct.
4. Vaccum distillation is used in liquids which decompose at their boiling Point. Hence (d) is incorrect.

Question: If a magnet is suspended at angle 30o to the magnetic meridian, the dip needle makes angle of 45o with the horizontal. The real dip is
Solution:
$$\tan {\delta ^1} = \frac{{\tan \delta }}{{\cos \theta }}$$
$$\tan \delta = \cos \theta \times \tan {\delta ^1}$$
$$\tan \delta = \cos {30^o} \times \tan {45^o}$$
$$\tan \delta = \frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{2}$$
$$\delta = {\tan ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{2}} \right)$$

Question: The momentum of a photon of energy 1 MeV in kg m/s, will be
Solution:
The energy of the photon,
$E = 1{\text{ MeV}} = 1 \times {10^6}{\text{ eV}}$
Where, Energy, $E = \frac{{hc}}{\lambda }$…… (1)
Now, from de-Broglie wavelength, $\lambda = \frac{h}{p}$…. (2)
Now, put the value of equation (2) in equation (1),
Therefore, $E = pc$
$ \Rightarrow p = \frac{E}{c}$
$ \Rightarrow p = \frac{{1 \times {{10}^6} \times 1.6 \times {{10}^{ - 19}}}}{{3 \times {{10}^8}}}$
$\therefore p = 5 \times {10^{ - 22}}{\text{ kg - m/s}}$

Question: Half-lives of two radioactive substances A and B are respectively 20 minutes and 40 minutes. Initially the samples of A and B have equal number of nuclei. After 80 minutes, the ratio of remaining numbers of A and B nuclei is
Solution:
${{\text{T}}_{\text{A}}}{\text{ = 20}}$ Min in 80 min, it has 4 half lives
${{\text{T}}_{\text{B}}}{\text{ = 40}}$ Min in 80 min, it has 2 half lives
So, \[{{\text{N}}_{\text{A}}}{\text{ = }}{{\text{N}}_{\text{0}}}{\left( {\frac{{\text{1}}}{{\text{2}}}} \right)^{\text{4}}}\]
$\begin{gathered}
  {{\text{N}}_{\text{B}}}{\text{ = }}{{\text{N}}_{\text{0}}}{\left( {\frac{{\text{1}}}{{\text{2}}}} \right)^{\text{2}}} \\
   \Rightarrow \frac{{{{\text{N}}_{\text{A}}}}}{{{{\text{N}}_{\text{B}}}}}{\text{ = }}\frac{{\frac{{\text{1}}}{{16}}}}{{\frac{1}{4}}} = \frac{1}{4} \\
\end{gathered} $

Question: Doubly ionised helium atoms and hydrogen ions are accelerated from rest through the same potential drop. The ratio of the final velocities of the helium and the hydrogen ions is
Solution:
Let, Mass of Hydrogen ion, mH, and mass of Helium ion, mHe = 4mH
Let, charge on Hydrogen ion, qH, and Helium ion qHe = 2qH
Therefore, the kinetic energy of the particle,
$K.E = \frac{1}{2}m{v^2} = qV$
$\therefore v \propto \sqrt {\frac{q}{m}} $
Now, the ratio of the final velocities of the Helium and Hydrogen ions, $\frac{{{v_{He}}}}{{{v_H}}} = \sqrt {\frac{{{q_{He}}}}{{{m_{He}}}} \cdot \frac{{{m_H}}}{{{q_H}}}} $
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{{v_{He}}}}{{{v_H}}} = \sqrt {\frac{{2{q_H}}}{{4{m_H}}} \cdot \frac{{{m_H}}}{{{q_H}}}} $
$\therefore \frac{{{v_{He}}}}{{{v_H}}} = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}$

Question: The ground state energy of hydrogen atom is – 13.6 eV. When its electron is in the first excited state, its excitation energy is
Solution:
Given, ground state energy of hydrogen atom,
\[{E_1} = - 13.6\,eV\]
Energy of electron in first excited state, i.e. n = 2
\[{E_2} = - \frac{{13.6\,}}{{{{(2)}^2}}}eV\]
Therefore, excitation energy,
\[\Delta E = {E_2} - {E_1} = - \frac{{13.6}}{4} - ( - 13.6)\]
\[\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, = - 3.4 + 13.6 = 10.2\,eV\]

Tags.Subject: Physics

Question: The vector $\widehat i + x\widehat j + 3\widehat k$ is rotated through an angle θ and doubled in magnitude, then it becomes $4\widehat i + \left( {4x - 2} \right)\widehat j + 2\widehat k.$ The possible positive value of x is
Solution:
$\sqrt {{4^2} + {{\left( {4x - 2} \right)}^2} + {2^2}} = 2\sqrt {{1^2} + {x^2} + {3^2}} $

On squaring, $16{x^2} - 16x + 24 - 4\left( {{x^2} + 10} \right)$

$ \Rightarrow \,\,3{x^2} - 4x - 4 = 0$

$ \Rightarrow \,\,\left( {3x + 2} \right)\left( {x - 2} \right) = 0$

$\therefore \,\,\,x = 2$ is positive value

Where, the wavelength of the radiation, $\lambda = 4100\mathop {\text{A}}\limits^o $
Therefore, $E = \frac{{12400}}{{4100}} = 3.02{\text{ eV}}$
The work function of the metals A, B, and C is $1.92{\text{ eV, }}2.0{\text{ eV, and }}5{\text{ eV}}$, respectively. If the work function of the metal is lower than the energy of incident light then photoelectrons will emit. So, metal C has a higher work function than the energy of incident light. Hence, only metal A and B
can emit photoelectrons.

Question: The mass number of He is 4 and that for sulphur is 32. The radius of sulphur nucleus is larger than that of helium by times

Using componendo and dividendo find out the value of x.
$\frac{{\sqrt {3x + 4} + \sqrt {3x - 5} }}{{\sqrt {3x + 4} - \sqrt {3x - 5} }} = 9$
Solution:
$ \Rightarrow \,\,\frac{{\left( {\sqrt {3x + 4} + \sqrt {3x - 5} } \right) + \left( {\sqrt {3x + 4} - \sqrt {3x - 5} } \right)}}{{\left( {\sqrt {3x + 4} + \sqrt {3x - 5} } \right) - \left( {\sqrt {3x + 4} - \sqrt {3x - 5} } \right)}}$

$ = \frac{{9 + 1}}{{9 - 1}}$

$ \Rightarrow \,\,\frac{{2\sqrt {3x + 4} }}{{2\sqrt {3x - 5} }} = \frac{{10}}{8} = \frac{5}{4}$

⇒ Simplifying and squaring both sides

$\frac{{3x + 4}}{{3x - 5}} = \frac{{25}}{{16}}$

$48x + 64 = 75x - 125$

$64 + 125 = 75x - 48x$

$189 = 27x$

$x = 7$

Question: Two  vectors \[\mathop A\limits^ \to  \,\,\,\,and\,\,\mathop B\limits^ \to  \,\,\,\,\,\] are such that \[\mathop A\limits^ \to  \,\,\,\, + \,\,\mathop B\limits^ \to  \,\,\,\,\, = \,\,\,\mathop C\limits^ \to  \,\,\]and \[{A^2}\,\, + \,\,{B^2}\,\, = \,\,{C^2}\]. If \[\theta \] is the angle between positive direction of  \[\mathop A\limits^ \to  \,\,\,\,and\,\,\mathop B\limits^ \to  \,\,\,\,\,\]then the correct statement is

An organic compound contains C, H, N, S and Cl. For the detection of chlorine, the sodium extract of the compound is first heated with a few drops of concentrated HNO<sub>3</sub> and then AgNO<sub>3</sub> is added to get a white ppt. of AgCl. The digestion with HNO<sub>3</sub> before the addition of AgNO<sub>3</sub> is
Options: to prevent the formation of NO<sub>2</sub>
to create a common ion effect
to convert CN<sup>-</sup> and S<sup>2-</sup> to volatile HCN and H<sub>2</sub>S, or else they will interfere with the test forming AgCN or Ag<sub>2</sub>S
to prevent the hydrolysis of NaCN and Na<sub>2</sub>S
Solution:
\[\begin{gathered}
  Na + C + N\xrightarrow{\Delta }NaCN \\
  2Na + S\xrightarrow{\Delta }N{a_2}S \\
\end{gathered} \]
Both NaCN & Na2S will react with AgNO3 to form \[\mathop {AgCN}\limits_{(white\;ppt)} \;\& \;A{g_2}s\left( {Black\;ppt} \right)\]. They must be removed before Performing AgNO3 test for halogens. Thus, on adding
\[\begin{gathered}
  {\text{HN}}{{\text{O}}_3},{\text{NaCN \& N}}{{\text{a}}_2}\;{\text{S}} \\
  {\text{NaCN}} + {\text{HN}}{{\text{O}}_3} \to {\text{NaN}}{{\text{O}}_3} + {\text{HCN}} \uparrow \\
  {\text{N}}{{\text{a}}_2}{\text{S}} + 2{\text{HN}}{{\text{O}}_3} \to 2{\text{NaN}}{{\text{O}}_3} + {{\text{H}}_2}{\text{S}} \uparrow \\
\end{gathered} \]
Hence, CN– & S–2 are removed as HCN & H2S by adding HNO3.
Hence, option (C) is correct.

Question: If vectors $\overrightarrow A $ and $\overrightarrow B $ have an angle θ between them, then value of \[\left| {\widehat A - \widehat B} \right|\]will be :
Solution:
If angle between $\vec A\,\,{\text{and}}\,\,\vec B\,\,{\text{is}}\,\,\theta $, then between \[\vec A\,\,{\text{and}} - \vec B\,\,{\text{is}}\,\,180^\circ - \theta \]
$|\hat A - \hat B| = \sqrt {{A^2} + {B^2} + 2\hat A\hat B\cos \left( {180^\circ - \theta } \right)} $
$ = \sqrt {1 + 1 + 2\left( { - \cos \theta } \right)} $
$ = \sqrt {2(1 - \cos \theta )} $
$ = \sqrt {2\left( {2{{\sin }^2}\frac{\theta }{2}} \right)} \left( {\because \cos 2\theta = 1 - 2{{\sin }^2}\theta } \right)$
$ = 2\sin \frac{\theta }{2}$

Question: The X and Y components of vector \[\vec A\] have numerical values 6 and 6 respectively and that of \[\left( {\vec A + \vec B} \right)\] have   numerical values 10 and 9. What is the numerical value of \[\vec B\]?
Solution:
\[\overrightarrow A = 6\widehat i + 6\widehat j\,;\,\,{\text{Let}}\,\overrightarrow B = x\widehat i + y\widehat j\]

\[\overrightarrow A + \overrightarrow B = 10\widehat i + 9\widehat j \Rightarrow \left( {6 + x} \right)\widehat i + \left( {6 + y} \right)\widehat j = 10\widehat i + 9\widehat j\]

Equating the components

\[ \Rightarrow 6 + x = 10\,\,{\text{and}}\,\,6 + y = 9\]

\[ \Rightarrow x = 4\,\,{\text{and}}\,\,y = 3\]

\[ \Rightarrow \overrightarrow B = 4\widehat i + 3\widehat j\]

\[ \Rightarrow \left| {\overrightarrow B } \right| = \sqrt {{4^2} + {3^2}} = 5\]

Question: What vector must be added to the sum of two vectors \[2\hat i - \hat j + 3\hat k\] and \[3\hat i - 2\hat j - 2\hat k\] so that the resultant is a unit vector along Z-axis
Solution:
\[\overrightarrow A = 2\widehat i - \widehat j + 3\widehat k;\overrightarrow B = 3\widehat i - 2\widehat j - 2\widehat k\]

Let a vector

\[\overrightarrow C = x\widehat i + y\widehat j + z\widehat k\,\,{\text{is}}\,\,{\text{added}}\,\,\overrightarrow A + \overrightarrow B + \overrightarrow C = \widehat k\]

\[ \Rightarrow \left( {5 + x} \right)\widehat i + \left( {y - 3} \right)\widehat j + \left( {1 + z} \right)\hat k = \hat k\]

Equating components.

\[5 + x = 0 \Rightarrow x = - 5\]

\[y - 3 = 0 \Rightarrow y = 3\]

\[1 + z = 1 \Rightarrow z = 0\]

\[ \Rightarrow \overrightarrow C = - 5\hat i + 3\hat j\]

Question: A bar magnet of length ‘L’ and magnetic dipole moment ‘M’ is bent in the form of an arc as shown in figure. The new magnetic dipole moment will be
Solution:
Given, \[\theta  = 60^\circ  = \frac{\pi }{3}rad\]
The new magnetic length of the dipole will be, \[l' = r\]
Now, \[M = m.l\] and \[M' = m.l'\]
\[\therefore \frac{M}{{M'}} = \frac{l}{{l'}}\,\,......\left( 1 \right)\]
Now, angle in radians \[ = \frac{{arc\,{\text{length}}}}{{{\text{radius}}}}\]
\[\therefore \frac{\pi }{3} = \frac{l}{r} \Rightarrow l = \frac{{\pi r}}{3}\]
In the equilateral triangle, marked by dotted lines, \[l' = r\]
\[\therefore \frac{l}{{l'}} = \frac{\pi }{3}\,.....\left( 2 \right)\]
From (1) and (2), we get, 
\[\frac{M}{{M'}} = \frac{\pi }{3}\]
\[\therefore M' = \frac{{3M}}{\pi }\]

Question: If the resultant of the vectors \[\left( {\hat i + 2\hat j - \hat k} \right),\,\,\left( {\hat i - \hat j + 2\hat k} \right)\] and $\overrightarrow C $ is a unit vector along the y-direction, then $\overrightarrow C $ is
Solution:
\[\overrightarrow A = \widehat i + 2\widehat j - \hat k;\,\overrightarrow B = \widehat i - \widehat j + 2\hat k\]

Let, \[\overrightarrow C = x\widehat i + y\widehat j + z\hat k\]

\[\overrightarrow A + \overrightarrow B + \overrightarrow C = 1\widehat j\]

\[ \Rightarrow \left( {2 + x} \right)\widehat i + \left( {1 + y} \right)\widehat j + \left( {1 + z} \right)\widehat k = 1\widehat j\]

Equating components.

\[2 + x = 0 \Rightarrow x = - 2\]

\[1 + y = 1 \Rightarrow y = 0\]

\[1 + z = 0 \Rightarrow z = - 1\]

\[ \Rightarrow \overrightarrow C = - 2\hat i - \hat k\]

Question: The half life of radium is about 1600 years. Of 100 g of radium existing now, 25 g will remain unchanged after
Solution:
$N = {N_0} \times {\left( {\frac{1}{2}} \right)^n}$
$ \Rightarrow 25 = 100 \times \left( {\frac{1}{2}} \right) \Rightarrow {\left( {\frac{1}{2}} \right)^n} = \frac{1}{4}$
$ \Rightarrow $ No. of half lives = n = 2
So, t = 2 × 1600 = 3200 yrs

Question:
Measure of two quantities along with the precision of respective measuring instrument is
A = 2.5 m s−1 ± 0.5 m s−1

B = 0.10 s ± 0.01 s

The value of AB will be
Solution:
A = (2.5 ± 0.5) ms−1

B = (0.10 ± 0.01) s

X = AB = 2.5 × 0.10 = 0.25 m

Δx/x = ΔA/A + ΔB/B

Δx/x = 0.5/2.5 + 0.01/0.10

Δx/x = 0.075/0.25, Δx = 0.007 ≅ 0.08

(Rounding off up to 2 significant figures)

∴ AB = (2.5 ± 0.08) m.

Question: Compare acidic strength:
Solution:
Benzoic acid is a stronger acid than phenol because the benzoate ion is stabilized by two equivalent resonance structures in which the negative charge is present at the more electronegative oxygen atom. Thus, the benzoate ion is more stable than phenoxide ion. Hence, benzoic acid is a stronger acid than phenol.
Phenol is more acidic than cyclohexanol and acyclic alcohols because the phenoxide ion is more stable than the alkoxide ion. In an alkoxide ion, such as the one derived from cyclohexanol, the negative charge is localized at the oxygen atom. Phenols substituted with electron-donating groups are less acidic than phenol. Hence the third option is correct.

Question:
Assertion: The pKa of acetic acid is lower than that of phenol.
Reason: Phenoxide ion is more resonance stabilised.
Options: Both Assertion and Reason are true and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are true but Reason is not the correct explanation of Assertion.
Assertion is true but Reason is false.
Both Assertion and Reason are false.
Solution:
Assertion: The${\text{p}}{{\text{K}}_{\text{a}}}$of acetic acid is lower than that of phenol.
Reason: Phenoxide ion is more resonance stabilised.

Higher the pKa, lower will be its acidity and vice-versa. Acetic acid has pKa of 2.4 whereas phenol has pKa of 10.

This value indicates that the carboxylate ion should be more stable than the phenoxide ion.

Also, phenoxide ion has less equivalent resonating structures than carboxylate ion.


Thus, the correct option is (c) Assertion is true but Reason is false

Question: Which of the following is the test of alcohol?

Sodium metal test: Add a small piece of dry sodium metal to it. Effervescence with the evolution of hydrogen gas indicate the presence of alcoholic group in the compounds.
Cerric ammonium nitrate test: To about 1 ml. of the substance in test tube add a few drops of cerric ammonium nitrate solution. Appearance of red colour indicate the presence of alcohols.
Ester test: When alcohol is treated with acetic acid in the presence of conc. H2SO4, a characteristic fruity smell due to the formation of ester indicates the presence of alcohol.

Question: Compare acidic strength:
Solution:
Benzoic acid is a stronger acid than phenol because the benzoate ion is stabilized by two equivalent resonance structures in which the negative charge is present at the more electronegative oxygen atom. Thus, the benzoate ion is more stable than phenoxide ion. Hence, benzoic acid is a stronger acid than phenol.
Phenol is more acidic than cyclohexanol and acyclic alcohols because the phenoxide ion is more stable than the alkoxide ion due to resonance stability. In an alkoxide ion, such as the one derived from cyclohexanol, the negative charge is localized at the oxygen atom.
Hence the c option is correct.

Question: Increasing order of acid strength among p-methoxyphenol, p-methylphenol and p-nitrophenol is
Solution:
The acidity of phenol and benzoic acids depends upon the electron density of the benzene ring. With increasing the electron deficiency of the benzene ring, the acidity increases and vice-versa.
Now, considering the electronic nature of the groups, the order of electron-withdrawing effect,
p-Nitrophenol> p-Methyl phenol> p-Methoxyphenol.
Therefore, increasing order of acid strength among p-methoxyphenyl, p-methyl phenol, and p-nitrophenol is, p-Nitrophenol> p-Methyl phenol> p-Methoxyphenol.

Reaction HCHO with a solution of NaOH
Reduction of CO using H<sub>2</sub> and ZnO–Cr<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>

The industrial method of preparation of methanol is the catalytic reduction of carbon monoxide. In this case, water gas is used. Water-gas is a mixture of equimolar CO and hydrogen gas.
In this case, a mixture of \[ZnO-C{r_2}{O_3}\] is used for the Reduction of CO using \[{H_2}\]. The reaction is shown below,
\[CO + 2{H_2}\xrightarrow{{ZnO - C{r_2}{O_3}}}C{H_3}OH\]

Question: Which of the following will produce a primary alcohol by reacting with CH<sub>3</sub>MgI followed by hydrolysis ?
Arrange the following compounds in increasing order of their reactivity in nucleophilic addition reactions. Ethanal (I), Propanal (II), Propanone (III), Butanone (IV)
Solution: The order of the reactivity towards the Nucleophilic addition reaction can be decided on the factor of electron density on the carbonyl compound. So, as the alkyl group increases the +I-effect increases and the electron density on the carbonyl group increases, this makes difficult for the nucleophile to attack. So, the order of reactivity will be Ethanal > Propanal > Propanone > Butanone.

Question: Which compound gives iodoform test?
Solution: Iodoform test is a chemical test that is used for detecting the presence of $C{H_3}CHOH - orC{H_3}CO - $ group in the molecule. When the compound is treated with iodine in the presence of sodium hydroxide, if there is formation of yellow precipitate, i.e., $CH{I_3}$, then the compound is having $C{H_3}CO - $ group. So, in the given options, $C{H_3}CHO$group, therefore, it will give the iodoform test.

Question: Which one of the following does not form sodium bisulphite addition product with sodium bisulphite solution
Solution: All aldehydes (aliphatic and aromatic) can react with sodium bisulphite and will give addition products. In ketones only methyl ketones react with sodium sulphite to form addition products. So, from the given options, acetophenone has bulkier groups on the carbonyl group, hence it will not react with sodium bisulfite

Question: An aldehyde on oxidation gives
Solution:
When the aldehydes or ketones are oxidized, there is formation of carboxylic acid. Or we can say that the carbonyl group of the aldehydes and ketones are converted into acid group in carboxylic acid. The reaction is:
     $R - CHO\mathop \to \limits^{\quad \left[ O \right]\quad } R - COOH$

Question:
The increasing order of the rate of HCN addition to compounds A-D is
(A) HCHO (B) CH3COCH3
(C) PhCOCH3 (D) PhCOPh
Solution:
We know that HCN is a good nucleophile so here the rate of the reaction will depend upon two main factors i.e. inductive effect and the steric factor. The low reactivity of the ketones over the aldehydes is due to the presence of +I group i.e. the alkyl group and the steric hindrance.
The aromatic aldehydes and ketones are less reactive than the aliphatic aldehydes due to the presence of +R effect of the benzene ring.
 Hence, the correct answer is option (c)

Question: Clemensen’s reduction of ketones is carried out in
Solution:
Clemmensen’s reduction is a common reaction which includes reduction of carbonyl compounds.
Here, zinc amalgam (which is nothing but a binary solution i.e. liquid in solid solution of zinc and mercury) along with concentrated hydrochloric acid is used for the reduction of ketones to give the corresponding hydrocarbon.
Therefore, the correct answer is option (d) Zn–Hg with HCl.

Question: The compound which reacts with hydroxyl amine but does not react with Tollen’s reagent is
Solution: Both the aldehydes and ketones can react with hydroxyl amine to form oximes. Tollen’s reagent is used to detect the presence of aldehyde in the compound, but ketones do not give the Tollen’s test. So, if the compound can react with hydroxyl amine but don’t give Tollen’s test, then it is a ketone.

Question: Which of the following will not undergo aldol condensation

So, the benzaldehyde whose formula is ${C_6}{H_5}CHO$ doesn’t have $\alpha - Hydrogen\;$so, it cannot show aldol condensation.

Question: The IUPAC name of CH<sub>3</sub>COCH (CH<sub>3</sub>)<sub>2</sub> is
Solution:
In the IUPAC nomenclature case, the root word is the number of total carbons in the longest chain of that compound.
No. of carbonsRoot word
1meth
2Eth
3Prop
4But
5Pent

… and so on.

The primary suffix is used to differentiate between the saturated compounds (Alkanes) and unsaturated compounds (Alkene and Alkynes).
compoundSuffix
AlkaneAne
AlkeneEne
AlkyneYne

If there is more than one suffix. Then one of those suffixes is considered as a secondary suffix.

Example: Methanol (Alkanol), here ‘ol’ is a secondary suffix.
The primary prefixes are used to differentiate between cyclic compound and noncyclic or chain compound. For cyclic compound prefix is ‘cyclo’. if there are any side chains or groups are present then secondary prefixes like ‘methyl’, ‘ethyl’ are used.
According to this rule, the IUPAC name is 3-methyl-2-butanone.

Question: Trichloroacetaldehyde was subjected to Cannizaro’s reaction by using NaOH. The mixture of the products contains sodium trichloroacetate ion and another compound. The other compound is

Question: In Wolf-Kishner reduction, the carbonyl group of aldehydes and ketones is converted into
Solution:

Here, the carbonyl functional group is converted to the methylene group by addition of $N{H_2} - N{H_2}$ by which we get hydrazone by the attack of the group to the carbonyl group. It is further reduced to an alkane by addition of ethylene glycol and heating.
Therefore, the correct answer is option (a) > CH2 group

Question: The key step in cannizzaro’s reaction is the intermolecular shift of
Solution:

In this reaction, shift of hydride ion takes place.
Therefore, the correct answer is option (b) Hydride ion.

Question: Catalyst used in Rosenmund reduction is
Solution:
The Rosenmund reaction is a hydrogenation process where molecular hydrogen reacts with acyl chloride in the presence of palladium/barium sulfate catalyst. Barium sulfate reduces the activity of palladium due to its low surface area, thereby preventing over reduction.
$R - CO - Cl\mathop \to \limits^{\quad {H_2} + Pd - BaS{O_4}\quad } R - CHO$

Question: Which compound undergoes iodoform reaction?
Solution:
Upon addition of iodine and sodium hydroxide to a compound containing a methyl ketone or any alcohol with a –Me group in the alpha position, formation of a yellow iodine precipitate takes place due to the formation of tri-iodomethane.
Therefore, the correct answer is option (b) $C{H_3}CHO$.

Question:

The most common method used for the preparation of acetophenone is Friedel Craft’s reaction in which the benzene is reacted with acetyl chloride and the product formed is acetophenone and hydrogen chloride. The reaction is given below:
${C_6}{H_6} + C{H_3}COCl \to {C_6}{H_5}COC{H_3} + HCl$
The other process from which we can produce acetophenone is oxidation of 1-Phenyl ethanol. The reaction is given below:
${C_6}{H_5}CHOHC{H_3}\mathop \to \limits^{\quad \left[ O \right]\quad } {C_6}{H_5}COC{H_3}$

Question: Correct order of reactivity of CH<sub>3</sub>CHO, C<sub>2</sub>H<sub>5</sub>COCH<sub>3</sub> and CH<sub>3</sub>COCH<sub>3</sub> is
Solution: Both the aldehydes and ketones are very reactive towards the Nucleophilic addition reaction, because carbonyl group is a very reactive group. So, the reactivity of the carbonyl group can be decided the magnitude of the positive charge on the carbonyl group. So, the compounds that have least +I effect and stearic hinderance group will have the highest positive charge and will have highest reactivity. So, the order will be: $C{H_3}CHO > C{H_3}COC{H_3} > C{H_3}CO{C_2}{H_5}$.

Solution:

The product formed here is known as Schiff’s base. We can write the reaction as \[{C_6}{H_5}CHO + {C_6}{H_5}N{H_2}\xrightarrow{{{H^ + }}}{C_6}{H_5}N = CH{C_6}{H_5} + {H_2}O\]Therefore, the correct answer is option (c) Schiff’s

Question: Which of the following will not give iodoform test?
Solution: Iodoform test is a chemical test that is used for detecting the presence of $C{H_3}CO - orC{H_3}CH\left( {OH} \right) - $group in the molecule. So, in the given options 3-pentanone whose formula is $C{H_3}C{H_2}COC{H_2}C{H_3}$, doesn’t have $C{H_3}CO - $group, so it will not give iodoform test.

Question:
Arrange the following compounds in decreasing order of their boiling points.
CH3CHO, CH3CH2OH, CH3OCH3, CH3CH2CH3
Solution: The boiling point of alcohols is the highest among the given compound, because of the formation of hydrogen bonding which is a strong bond. Then comes the aldehydes which have higher boiling point than hydrocarbons and ethers, since they are polar compounds and have higher dipole –dipole interactions. Among ethers and hydrocarbons, ethers have the higher boiling point and the hydrocarbons have the least boiling point due to weak vander Waals interactions. So the correct order of compounds on the decreasing order of boiling points is as follows: $C{H_3}C{H_2}OH > C{H_3}CHO > C{H_3}OC{H_3} > C{H_3}C{H_2}C{H_3}$

Question: Ethanedial has which functional group ?
Solution: Ethanedial or Glyoxal has two aldehyde group.

Question: The most reactive compound towards formation of cyanohydrin on treatment with HCN followed by acidification is
Solution: Cyanohydrin will be formed easily by those compounds in which electron withdrawing group is present. Electron withdrawing will have –I-Effect which will have more positive charge on the carbonyl carbon atom decreasing the electron density and increasing the tendency of the HCN to attack. So, from the given options in p-nitrobenzaldehyde, nitro group is electron withdrawing group.
This is example of Cannizaro reaction.

Solution:
When acetaldehyde is treated with the Fehling solution which is tartaric complex of the cuprous ions, a red precipitate of $C{u_2}O$ is obtained. The reaction involved is:

Hence, the correct answer is option (c)

Question: The formula of chloral is
Solution: The IUPAC name of chloral is 3,3,3-Trichloroethanal. There are two carbon atoms in the given compound and there are two functional groups in the compound, these are aldehyde and chlorine. So, the formula of chloral is $\;CC{l_3}CHO$.

Question: Tollen’s reagent is
Options: alkaline solution containing complex of copper nitrate
Solution: Ammoniacal AgNO<sub>3</sub> is Tollen’s reagent.

Question: The correct sequence of step involved in the mechanism of Cannizzaro’s reaction is
Options: nucleophilic attack, transfer of H<sup>–</sup>  and transfer of H<sup>+</sup>.
transfer of H<sup>–</sup>, transfer of H<sup>+</sup> and nucleophilic attack.
transfer of H<sup>+</sup>, nucleophilic attack and transfer of H<sup>–</sup>.
electrophilic attack by OH<sup>–</sup>, transfer of H<sup>+</sup> and transfer of H<sup>–</sup>.
Solution: Cannizzaro’s reaction is a chemical property of the aldehydes. The aldehydes that do not have $\alpha - Hydrogen$ can undergo disproportionation reaction, i.e., self oxidation and reduction reaction. The mechanism involved in the Cannizzaro’s reaction is first Nucleophilic attack (fast reaction), then hydride transfer (slow step), and then ${H^ + }$ transfer.

Question: Benedict’s solution is not reduced by
Solution: Benedict’s solution is a test for the presence of aldehyde group in the compound. It works same as Fehling’s solution. So, from the given options, acetic anhydride is not an aldehyde therefore, it will not give the benedict’s test.

Question: Aldehydes and ketones are differentiated by using
Solution: Tollen’s test is known as silver mirror test and it is used for the detection of aldehyde in the compound. Ammonical solution of silver nitrate is known as Tollen’s reagent, and when an aldehyde reacts with Tollen’s reagent then there is formation of silver mirror. The reactant used in Tollen’s test is AgNO3/NH4OH. Since this test is only given by the aldehydes, it can be used to differentiate between aldehyde and ketone.

Question: The general formula of both aldehydes and ketones is:
Solution: Let us take an aldehyde, ethanal. The formula of ethanal is $C{H_3}CHO$, so the number of carbon atoms is 2 and the number of hydrogen atoms is 4, and the number of oxygen atom is one. Now let us take a ketone, acetone. The formula of acetone is $C{H_3}COC{H_3}$, so the number of carbon atoms is 3, the number of hydrogen atoms is 6, and the number of oxygen atom is one. So, from this we can conclude the general formula of aldehydes and ketones as ${C_n}{H_{2n}}O$.

Question: Aldehydes and ketones can be reduced to corresponding hydrocarbons by
Options: Refluxing with soda amalgam and water
Refluxing with zinc amalgam and concentrated HCl
Solution:
Reduction of aldehydes and ketones to their corresponding hydrocarbons is carried out by a reaction named Clemmenson’s reduction.
Here, zinc amalgam (which is nothing but a binary solution i.e. liquid in solid solution of zinc and mercury) along with concentrated hydrochloric acid is used for the reduction of ketones to give the corresponding hydrocarbon.
Therefore, the correct answer is option (d) refluxing with zinc amalgam and concentrated HCl

Hence, the correct answer is option (a)

Question: Aldehydic group can occur:

only at the second carbon atom of the chain
only at the end carbon atom of the carbon chain

Question: Which of the following is paradehyde?
The IUPAC name of Cl<sub>3</sub>CCH<sub>2</sub>CHO is :
Solution: The IUPAC name is given by taking the longest chain of the carbon atoms. There are three carbon atoms in the given compound and there are two functional groups in the compound, and these are aldehyde and chlorine. The numbering will start from aldehyde. So, the name of the compound will be 3,3,3 Trichloropropanal.

Question: Which one of the following pairs is not correctly matched
Solution:
In Clemmensen’s reduction, alkanes are formed from aldehyde or ketone with zinc amalgam and concentrated hydrochloric acid. So, the option (a) is correct.
In Wolf- Kishner reduction, alkanes are formed from aldehyde or ketone when they are heated with hydrazine and KOH. So, the option (b) is incorrect.
In Rosenmund’s reduction, the aldehyde is formed from acid chloride, when acid chloride is treated with hydrogen in the presence of boiling xylene solution and over Pd or $BaS{O_4}$surface which is used as the catalyst and they are also called as the Rosenmund’s catalyst. So, option (c) is correct.
In Stephen reduction, the aldehyde is formed from nitriles, by treating the nitriles with stannous chloride in the presence of hydrochloric acid. So, option (d) is correct.

Question: Which of the following is the industrial method of preparation of acetaldehyde?
Solution:
Acetaldehyde is an organic compound having formula $C{H_3}CHO\;$and industrially it is prepared from Wacker process, in which the alkene is treated with water in the presence of palladium chloride or copper chloride. Acetaldehyde is easily prepared from ethene. Since palladium chloride is an expensive catalyst so, copper chloride is mostly used. so copper chloride is used for regeneration of palladium.
$C{H_2} = C{H_2} + PdC{l_2} + {H_2}O\mathop \to \limits^{\quad CuC{l_2}{O_2}\quad } C{H_3}CHO + Pd + 2HCl$
$\left[ {Pd + 2HCl \to PdC{l_2} + {H_2}} \right]$ $\left[ {Pd + 2CuC{l_2} \to PdC{l_2} + 2CuCl} \right]$ $\left[ {2CuCl + \frac{1}{2}{O_2} + 2HCl \to 2CuC{l_2} + {H_2}O} \right]$

Question: What reagent is used in the Rosenmund reduction?
Solution:
Rosenmund reaction or Rosenmund reduction is a chemical reaction in which the acid chlorides are converted into aldehydes. The reactant in the Rosenmund reaction is acid chloride, and it is reacted with hydrogen in the presence of palladium supported over barium sulfate so, the formation of aldehyde will take place and hydrochloric acid is also produced. The reaction is given below:
$R - CO - Cl\mathop \to \limits^{\quad {H_2} + Pd - BaS{O_4}\quad } R - CHO$

Question: Which of the aldehyde is most reactive towards nucleophilic addition reaction ?
Solution: Both aldehydes and ketones are very reactive towards the Nucleophilic addition reaction, because carbonyl group is a very reactive group. So, the reactivity of the carbonyl group can be decided the magnitude of the positive charge on the carbonyl group. So, the compounds that have least +I and +R group will have the highest positive charge and will have highest reactivity. So, the HCHO doesn’t have any +I and +R group, therefore, it will have the highest reactivity.

\[\left( {\because \,\,{I_{rms}} = \frac{{{I_0}}}{{\sqrt 2 }} = \frac{{220/110}}{{\sqrt 2 }} = \sqrt 2 } \right)\]

Question: The power factor in a circuit connected to an AC power supply has a value which is
Options: unity when the circuit contains only inductance
unity when the circuit contains only resistance
zero when the circuit contains an ideal resistance only
unity when the circuit contains an ideal capacitance only
Solution:
Power factor, \[\cos \phi = \frac{R}{Z}\]
When circuit contains only resistance,
Then Z = R
\[\therefore \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\cos \phi = \frac{R}{R} = 1\]

Question:
Assertion : When an electric lamp connected in series with a  variable capacitor and ac source, its brightness decreases with increase in capacitance.
Reason: Capacitive reactance increases with increase in capacitance of capacitor.
Options: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and the Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but the Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is true, but Reason is false.
Both Assertion and Reason are false.
Solution: Capacitive reactance \[{X_C} = \frac{1}{{\omega C}}\] when capacitance increases, the capacitive reactance decrease. Due to decrease in its value, the current in the circuit will increase \[\left( {I = \frac{E}{{\sqrt {{R^2} + X_C^2} }}} \right)\] and hence brightness of source (or electric lamp) will also increases.

Question: A transistor-oscillator using a resonant circuit with an inductor L (of negligible resistance) and a capacitor C in series produce oscillations of frequency f. If $L$ is doubled and C is changed to 4C, the frequency will be:
Solution:
Inductor $L$, capacitor $C$, and frequency $f$Now, in a series LC circuit, the frequency of oscillation is,
 $f = \frac{1}{{2\pi \sqrt {LC} }}$
$ \Rightarrow f \propto \frac{1}{{\sqrt {LC} }}$
Now, according to question, the inductor is doubled,
 $L' = 2L$
And capacitance,
 $C' = 4C$
 Therefore, new frequency,
 $f' = \frac{1}{{2\pi \sqrt {2L \times 4C} }} = \frac{1}{{2\pi \sqrt {8LC} }}$
Now, the ratio of newer upon the older frequency,
$\frac{{f'}}{f} = \frac{1}{{2\pi \sqrt {8LC} }} \times \frac{{2\pi \sqrt {LC} }}{1} = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 8 }} = \frac{1}{{2\sqrt 2 }}$
$\therefore f' = \frac{f}{{2\sqrt 2 }}$

Question: A transformer has 1500 turns in the primary coil and 1125 turns in the secondary coil. If the voltage in the primary coil is 200 V, then the voltage in the secondary coil is

An inductor L, a capacitor of 20 µFand a resistor of 10 Ω are connected in series with an AC source of frequency 50 Hz. If the current is in phase with the voltage, then the inductance of the inductor is
Solution:
In an L-C-R circuit, the current and the voltage are in phase (φ = 0), when
\[\tan \phi = \frac{{\omega L - \frac{1}{{\omega C}}}}{R} = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \,\,\,\,\omega L = \frac{1}{{\omega C}} \Rightarrow L = \frac{1}{{{\omega ^2}C}}\]
Here, ω = 2πf
= 2 × 3.14 × 50 s-1 = 314 s-1
And C = 20 μF = 20 × 10-6 F
\[\therefore \,L = \frac{1}{{{{\left( {314\,{s^{ - 1}}} \right)}^2} \times \left( {20 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}F} \right)}} = 0.51\,H\]

Question: A coil of resistance 200 ohm and self-inductance 1.0 henry has been connected to an a.c. source of frequency 200/π Hz. The phase difference between voltage and current is
Solution:
From phasor diagram,
 \[\tan \theta = \frac{{\omega L}}{R} = \frac{{2\pi fL}}{R} = \frac{{2\pi \times \frac{{200}}{\pi } \times 1}}{{200}}\]
\[\therefore \theta = {\tan ^{ - 1}}\left( 2 \right)\]
\[\theta = 63^\circ \]

\[{{\text{V}}_{{\text{ac}}}}{\text{ = }}\sqrt {{\text{V}}_{\text{C}}^{\text{2}}{\text{ + V}}_{\text{R}}^{\text{2}}} {\text{ = }}\sqrt {{{\text{6}}^{\text{2}}}{\text{ + }}{{\text{8}}^{\text{2}}}} {\text{ = 10V}}\]
The circuit is capacitive in nature
\[\cos \phi = \frac{8}{{10}} = 0.8\]

Question: In an LCR series a.c. circuit, the voltage across each of the components, L, C and R is \[50\;{\text{V}}\]. The voltage across the LC combination will be:
Solution: In an LCR series a.c. circuit, the voltage across inductor L leads the current by 90 degree and the voltage across capacitor C lags behind the current by \[900\]. Hence, the voltage across LC combination will be zero.

Question: An AC source of frequency 50 Hz is connected in series to an inductance of 0.5 H and resistance of 157Ω. The phase difference between current and voltage is
Solution:
\[\tan \phi = \frac{{{X_L}}}{R} = \frac{{\omega L}}{R} = \frac{{2\pi fL}}{R}\]
\[ \Rightarrow \,\,\,\,\,\tan \phi = \frac{{2 \times 3.14 \times 50 \times 0.5}}{{157}} = 1\]
\[ \Rightarrow \,\,\,\,\,\phi = 45^\circ \]

Question:
Assertion: The voltage and current in LCR circuit is in the same phase at resonance.
Reason: At resonance, inductive reactance and capacitive reactance has zero value.
Options: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and the Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but the Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is true, but Reason is false.
Both Assertion and Reason are false.
Solution: Phase angle is given by $\tan \phi \,\, = \,\,\frac{{{V_L}\,\, - \,\,{V_C}}}{{{V_R}}}\,\, = \,\,\frac{{{X_L}\,\, - \,\,{X_C}}}{R};$ at resonance, ${X_L}\,\, = \,\,{X_C};$so $\tan \phi \,\, = \,\,0$ and voltage and current become in same phase.

Question: In the series LCR circuit, the voltmeter and ammeter readings are :
Solution:
From phasor diagram we can say that the net voltage due to inductor and capacitor is 0.
$\therefore \,{V_R} = {V_{ac}} = 100V$
Also $V = iR$
$\therefore \,\frac{{100}}{{50}} = i$
$\therefore \,i = 2A$

Question: A transformer with efficiency 80% works at 4 kW and 100 V. If the secondary voltage is 200 V, then the primary and secondary currents are respectively,
Solution:
Efficiency, \[\eta = \frac{{output\,power}}{{input\,power}}\]
\[ \Rightarrow \,\,\,\,\,\,\eta = \frac{{{V_s}{I_s}}}{{{V_p}{I_p}}}\]
\[ \Rightarrow \,\,\,\,\,\,\frac{{80}}{{100}} = \frac{{200 \times {I_s}}}{{4000}}\]
\[ \Rightarrow \,\,\,\,\,\,{I_s} = 16\,A\]
Also, VpIp = 4000
\[ \Rightarrow \,\,\,\,\,\,{I_p} = \frac{{4000}}{{100}} = 40\,A\]

Question: In the given circuit, if the reading of voltmeter V1 and V2 are 300 volts each, then the reading of the voltmeter V3 and ammeter A are respectively
Solution:
As \[V = \sqrt {V_R^2 + {{\left( {{V_L} - {V_C}} \right)}^2}} \]
Here,
\[\begin{gathered}
  V = 220\,V \\
  {V_L} = {V_C} = 300V \\
  \therefore \,\,220V\,\, = \sqrt {V_R^2 + {{\left( {300V - 300V} \right)}^2}} \\
  or\,\,\,{V_R} = 220V \\
\end{gathered} \]
Hence the reading of voltmeter V3 is 220 V.
\[I = \frac{{{V_R}}}{R} = \frac{{220V}}{{100\Omega }} = 2.2A\]
Hence the reading of ammeterV3 is 22 A.

Question: What is the value of inductance L for which the current is a maximum in a series L-C-R circuit with C = 10µF and ω = 1000 s<sup>-1</sup> ?
Options: Cannot be calculated unless R is known
Solution:
Current in L-C-R series circuit is given by
\[i = \frac{V}{{\sqrt {{R^2} + {{\left( {{X_L} - {X_C}} \right)}^2}} }}\]
where, V is rms value of current, R is resistance, XL is inductive reactance and XC is capacitive reactance. For current to be maximum, denominator should be minimum which can be done, if XL = XC. This happens in resonance state of the circuit,
i.e. \[\omega L = \frac{1}{{\omega C}} \Rightarrow L = \frac{1}{{{\omega ^2}C}}\]…(i)
Given, \[\omega = 1000\,{s^{ - 1}}\]
And \[C = 10\mu F = 10 \times {10^{ - 6}}F.\]
Hence,
\[L = \frac{1}{{{{\left( {1000} \right)}^2} \times 10 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}}} = 0.1\,H = 100\,mH\]

Question: In an L-C-R circuit R = 100 Ω. When capacitance C is removed, the current lags behind the voltage by π/3, when inductance L is removed, the current leads the voltage by π/3. The impedance of the circuit is
Solution:
When C is removed, circuit becomes R-L circuit
Hence, \[\tan \frac{\pi }{3} = \frac{{{X_L}}}{R}\] …(i)
When L is removed, circuit becomes R-C circuit.
Hence \[\tan \frac{\pi }{3} = \frac{{{X_C}}}{R}\] …(ii)
From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get
XL = XC, this is the condition of resonance, Z = R = 100 Ω.

Question: Alternating voltage V = 400 sin (500 π t) is applied across a resistance of 0.2 k Ω. The r.m.s. value of current will be equal to
Solution:
\[V = 400\sin \left( {500\pi t} \right)\]
\[i = \frac{V}{R} = \frac{{400}}{{0.2 \times {{10}^3}}}\sin \left( {500\pi t} \right)\]
\[i = 2\sin \left( {500\pi t} \right)\]
\[{i_{rms}} = \frac{2}{{\sqrt 2 }} = \sqrt 2 = 1.414{\text{ }}A\]

Question: The instantaneous voltage through a device of impedance 20Ω is e = 80 sin 100 πt. The effective value of the current is
Solution:
The instantaneous voltage through the given device, e = 80 sin 100 πt .
Comparing the given instantaneous voltage with standard instantaneous voltage
e = e0sin ωt, we get
e0 = 80 V
where, e0 is the peak value of voltage. Impedance
(Z) = 20 Ω
Peak value of current,
\[{I_0} = \frac{{{e_0}}}{Z} = \frac{{80}}{{20}} = 4A\]
Effective value of current (root mean square value of current),
\[{I_{rms}} = \frac{{{I_0}}}{{\sqrt 2 }} = \frac{4}{{\sqrt 2 }} = 2\sqrt 2 = 2.828\,A\]

Question: An rms voltage of \[110{\mkern 1mu} \,V\] is applied across a series circuit having resistance $11\,\Omega $ and impedance $22\,\Omega $. The power consumed is
Solution:
$P = {V_{{\text{rms}}}}{I_{{\text{rms}}}}\cos \phi $
\[ = {V_{{\text{rms}}}}\left( {\frac{{{V_{{\text{rms}}}}}}{Z}} \right)\left( {\frac{R}{Z}} \right) = \frac{{{{\left( {110} \right)}^2}\left( {11} \right)}}{{{{\left( {22} \right)}^2}}} = 275\,W\]

Question:
In an AC circuit, the emf (e) and the current (i) at any instant are given respectively by
\[e = {E_0}\sin \omega t\,\,\, \Rightarrow \,\,i = {I_0}\sin \left( {\omega t - \phi } \right)\]
The average power in the circuit over one cycle of AC is
Solution:
Given, e = E0sinωt
and i = I0 sin(ωt - φ)
Instantaneous power, Pint = ei
= E0I0 sinωt sin(ωt - φ)
Average power over one cycle is
\[{P_{av}} = rac{1}{T}\sum olimits_0^T {{P_{\operatorname{int} }}dt} \]
\[ = rac{1}{T}\sum olimits_0^T {{E_0}{I_0}} \sin \omega t\sin \left( {\omega t - \phi } ight)dt\]
\[ = rac{1}{T} imes {E_0}{I_0}\sum olimits_0^T {\left( {{{\sin }^2}\omega t\cos \phi + \sin \phi \sin \omega t\cos \omega t} ight)dt} \]
\[\, = rac{{{E_0}{I_0}}}{T}\left[ {\cos \phi imes rac{T}{2} \pm 0} ight]\]
\[ = rac{{\left( {{E_0}{I_0}\cos \phi } ight)T/2}}{T} = rac{{{E_0}{I_0}\cos \phi }}{2}\]
where, cosφ is called the power factor of an AC circuit.

Question: A series LCR circuit is tuned to resonance. The impedance of the circuit now is
Solution:
\[Z = \sqrt {{R^2} + {{\left( {\omega L - \frac{1}{{\omega c}}} \right)}^2}} \]
At resonance \[\omega L = \frac{1}{{\omega c}}\]
\[Z = R\]

Question:
Assertion: Quality factor (Q-factor) of series resonant circuit is a unitless quantity
Reason: It defines sharpness of i-V curve at resonance in LCR series circuit.
Options: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and the Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but the Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is true, but Reason is false.
Both Assertion and Reason are false.
Solution:
It defines sharpness of $i - V$ curve at resonance when Q-factor is large, the sharpness of resonance curve is more and vice-versa. Q-factor is also defined as follows,
\[Q - factor\,\, = \,\,2\pi \, \times \frac{{Max.\,\,energy\,\,stored}}{{Energy\,\,dissipation}}\]
\[ = \,\,\frac{{2\pi }}{T}\, \times \,\frac{{Max.\,\,energy\,\,stored}}{{Mean\,\,power\,\,dissipated}}\]
\[ = \,\,\frac{{\operatorname{Re} sonant\,\,frequency}}{{Band\,\,width}}\,\, = \,\,\frac{{{\omega _o}}}{{\Delta \omega }};\,\,that\,\,is,\,\,unitless.\]

Question: A current of $ 5\,\text{A} $ is flowing at $ 220\,\text{V} $ in the primary coil of a transformer. If the voltage produced in the secondary coil is $ 2200\,\text{V} $ and 50% of power is lost, then the current in the secondary coil will be
Solution:
Given that $ {{V}_{p}}=220\,\text{V; }{{I}_{p}}=5\,A;{{V}_{S}}=2200\,\text{V} $ and $ {{p}_{S}}=\frac{{{P}_{p}}}{2} $
According to data we can write, $ \frac{{{V}_{p}}{{I}_{p}}}{2}={{V}_{S}}{{I}_{S}}\Rightarrow {{I}_{S}}=\frac{{{V}_{p}}}{{{V}_{s}}}\times \frac{{{I}_{p}}}{2}=\frac{220}{2200}\times \frac{5}{2}=0\cdot 25\,\text{A} $

Question: The impedance of a circuit, when a resistance R and an inductor of inductance L are connected in series in an AC circuit of frequency f, is
Solution:
In L-R circuit,
Impedance, \[Z = \sqrt {{R^2} + X_L^2} \]
Here, \[{X_L} = \omega L = 2\pi fL\]
\[ \Rightarrow \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,Z = \sqrt {{R^2} + 4{\pi ^2}{f^2}{L^2}} \]

Question: In an electrical circuit $ R,L,C $ and an $ a.c. $ voltage source are all connected in series. When $ L $ is removed from the circuit, the phase difference between the voltage and the current in the circuit is $ \pi /3. $ If instead, $ C $ is removed from the circuit, the phase difference is again $ \pi /3. $ The power factor of the circuit is
Solution:
when $ L $ is removed from the circuit

 $ \frac{{{X_C}}}{R} = \tan \frac{\pi }{3} $
 $ {X_C} = R\tan \frac{\pi }{3} $ …(1)
When $ C $ is remove from the circuit

 $ \frac{{{X_L}}}{R} = \tan \frac{\pi }{3} $
 $ {X_C} = R\tan \frac{\pi }{3} $ …(2)

net impedance $ Z = \sqrt {{R^2} + {{({X_L} - {X_C})}^2} = R} $
power factor $ \cos \phi = \frac{R}{Z} = 1 $

Question: An ideal choke draws a current of 8 A when connected to an AC supply of 100 V, 50 Hz. A pure resistor draws a current of 10 A when connected to the same source. The ideal choke and the resistor are connected in series and then connected to the AC source of 150 V, 40 Hz. The current in the circuit becomes
Solution:
Resistance, \[R = \frac{{100}}{{10}} = 10\Omega \]
Inductive reactance, XL = 2πfL
\[\frac{{100}}{8} = 2\pi \times 50 \times L\]
\[ \Rightarrow \,\,L = \frac{1}{{8\pi }}H\]
\[{X'_L} = 2\pi f'L\]
\[ = 2\pi \times 40 \times \frac{1}{{8\pi }} = 10\Omega \]
Impedance of the circuit,
\[Z = \sqrt {{R^2} + X_L^2}  = \sqrt {{{\left( {10} \right)}^2} + {{\left( {10} \right)}^2}}  = 10\sqrt 2 {\mkern 1mu} \Omega \]
Current in the circuit,
\[i = \frac{V}{Z} = \frac{{150}}{{10\sqrt 2 }} = \frac{{15}}{{\sqrt 2 }}A\]

Question: If a transformer of an audio amplifier has output impedance 8000 Ω and the speaker has input impedance of 8 Ω, the primary and secondary turns of this transformer connected between the output of amplifier and to loudspeaker should have the ratio
Solution:
From Faraday’s law, the induced emf across primary and secondary is
\[{e_p} = - {N_p}\frac{{\Delta \phi }}{{\Delta t'}}\]
\[{e_s} = - {N_s}\frac{{\Delta \phi }}{{\Delta t}}\]
Also, e = iR
\[\therefore \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\frac{{{R_p}}}{{{R_s}}} = \frac{{{N_p}}}{{{N_s}}}\]
Given, Rs = 8000Ω, Rp = 8Ω
\[\therefore \,\,\,\,\,\frac{{{N_s}}}{{{N_p}}} = \frac{{{R_s}}}{{{R_p}}} = \frac{{8000}}{8} = \frac{{1000}}{1} = 1000:1\]

Question: In a L-R circuit, the value of L is $\left( {\frac{{0.4}}{\pi }} \right)\;{\text{henry}}$ and the value of R is 30 ohm. If in the circuit, an alternating emf of 200 volt at 50 cycles per sec is connected, the impedance of the circuit and current will be:
The inductive reactance of a coil of \[0.2{\text{H}}\] inductance at a frequency of \[60\;{\text{Hz}}\] is
Solution:
Inductance \[ = 2\pi fL = 2 \times 3.14 \times 60 \times 0.2 = 75.4\Omega \]


Video Link:

Question:
Read the assertion and reason carefully to mark the correct option out of the options given below.
Assertion: When capacitive reactance is smaller than the inductive reactance in LCR current, emf leads the current.
Reason: The phase angle is the angle between the alternating emf and alternating current of the circuit.
Options: If both assertion and reason are true and the reason is the correct explanation of the assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true, but the reason is not the correct explanation of the assertion.
If assertion is true, but reason is false.
If both the assertion and reason are false.
Solution:
The phase angle for the LCR circuit is given by
$\tan \varphi = \frac{{{X_L} - {X_C}}}{R} = \frac{{\omega L - 1/\omega C}}{R}$
Where XL, XC are inductive reactance and capacitive reactance respectively when XL > XC then tanφ is positive i.e. φ is positive (between 0 and π /2). Hence emf leads the current.

Question:
In the circuit shown in figure the r.m.s. value of e is 5 V and r.m.s. value of voltage drop across L is 3 V.

The r.m.s. value of voltage across R will be
Solution:
From diagram we can say that,
$V = \sqrt {{V_R}^2 + {V_L}^2} $
$5 = \sqrt {{V_R}^2 + {3^2}} $
VR = 4V

Question: In L-C-R resonant circuit, what is the phase angle φ ?
Solution:
In an L-C-R circuit, phase angle is related as \[\cos \phi = \frac{R}{Z}\]
Where, \[Z = \sqrt {{R^2} + {{\left( {{X_L} - {X_C}} \right)}^2}} \]
When circuit is resonant,
\[{X_L} = {X_C}\,\,\,\, \Rightarrow \,\,\,Z = R\]
So, \[\cos \phi = \frac{R}{R} = 1,\,\,\phi = 0^\circ \]

Question: If an electric current i as a function of time t is given as i = t<sup>2</sup> (for $0 < t \leqslant T$), then RMS value of the current for time 0 to T is
Solution:
$$i = {t^2}.$$
Heat produced in time 'T'
$$Q = \int_0^T {{i^2}Rdt} $$
$$ = R\int_0^T {{t^4}dt} $$
$$Q = R\frac{{{T^5}}}{5}$$
Again $$i_{rms}^2RT = \frac{{R{T^5}}}{5}$$
$${i_{rms}} = \sqrt {\frac{{{T^4}}}{5}} $$
or, $${i_{rms}} = \frac{{{T^2}}}{{\sqrt 5 }}$$

Tags.Subject: Physics
Tags.Topic: Average, Peak and RMS Value of Alternating Current/Voltage

Question: The instantaneous values of current and voltage in an AC circuit are given by I = 6 sin(100𝜋t + 𝜋/4), V = 5 sin(100 𝜋t - 𝜋/4), then
Solution: (c) current leads the voltage by 90°

Question: An alternating current of rms value 10 A is passed through a 12 Ω resistor. The maximum potential difference across the resistor is
Solution:
Given, Irms = 10 A
And R = 12 Ω
\[\therefore \,{E_{rms}} = {I_{rms}} \times R = 10 \times 12 = 120\,V\]
Now, maximum potential difference
\[{E_0} = {E_{rms}} \times \sqrt 2 = 120 \times \sqrt 2 = 169.68\,V\]

Tags.Subject: Physics
Tags.Topic: Average, Peak and RMS Value of Alternating Current/Voltage

Question: An alternating emf given by equation $e = 300\sin \left[ {\left( {100\pi } \right)t} \right]{\text{V}}$ is applied to a resistance $100\Omega $. The rms current through the circuit is (in amperes)
Solution:
The given equation, $e = 300\sin \left[ {\left( {100\pi } \right)t} \right]$ ….. (i)
We know that, $e = {e_0}\sin \omega t$ …… (ii)
On comparing Eqs (i) and (ii), we get
     ${e_0} = 300{\text{V}}$and $R = 100\Omega $
The rms current through the circuit ${{\text{I}}_{{\text{rms}}}} = ?$
\[{{\text{I}}_{{\text{rms}}}} = \frac{{{e_0}}}{{\sqrt 2 R}} = \frac{{300}}{{\sqrt 2 \times 100}} = \frac{3}{{\sqrt 2 }}{\text{A}}\]

Tags.Subject: Physics
Tags.Topic: Average, Peak and RMS Value of Alternating Current/Voltage

Question: In L-C-R series A.C. circuit, the phase angle between current and voltage is
Solution:
In L.C.R series AC circuit the phase angle between
current and the voltage is,
 \[{V_L} - {V_C} = i\left( {{X_L} - {X_C}} \right),\,\tan \phi = \frac{{{X_L} - {X_C}}}{R}\]
If \[{V_L} > {V_C}\]
\[ \Rightarrow 0 < \phi < \frac{\pi }{2}\]
If \[{V_L} < {V_C}\]
\[ \Rightarrow - \frac{\pi }{2} < \phi < 0\]
\[\therefore \left| \phi \right| \in \left[ {0,\frac{\pi }{2}} \right]\]

Question: Same current is flowing in two alternating circuits. The first circuit contains only inductance and the other contains only a capacitor. If the frequency of the emf of AC is increased, the effect on the value of the current will be
Options: increases in the first circuit and decreases in the other
decreases in the first circuit and increases in the second circuit
Solution:
We know that current in purely inductive circuit and purely capacitive circuit is given by respectively,
\[{I_L} = \frac{{{E_0}}}{{\omega L}}\sin \left( {\omega t - \pi /2} \right)\]
And \[{I_C} = \frac{{{E_0}}}{{1/\omega C}}\sin \left( {\omega t + \pi /2} \right)\]
\[ = {E_0}\sin \omega t\left( {\omega t + \pi /2} \right) \times \omega C\]
Here, ω is the angular frequency of the alternating current. Thus, current will decrease in the first circuit and increase in second circuit.

Question: The ratio of specific heats $\left( {\frac{{{C_p}}}{{{C_V}}}} \right)$ in terms of degree of freedom (f) is given by:
Solution:
Cp – Cv = R

\[{C_v} = \frac{f}{2}R\]

f = degree of freedom

\[{C_p} = R + {C_v} = R + \frac{f}{2}R = R\left( {1 + \frac{f}{2}} \right)\]


\[ = \frac{2}{f}\left( {1 + \frac{f}{2}} \right) = \left( {1 + \frac{2}{f}} \right)\]

Tags.Subject: Physics
Tags.Topic: Law of Equipartition of Energy and Specific Heat Capacity

Tags.Subject: Physics
Tags.Topic: Kinetic Theory of an Ideal Gas
